title: Geometric Integrators for Nonholonomic Systems on Lie Groups
comment: Submitted to IEEE Control Systems Letters in conjunction with the 65th IEEE Conference on Decision and Control
summary: We present a general framework for constructing structure-preserving numerical integrators for nonholonomically constrained mechanical systems evolving on Lie groups using retraction maps. Retraction maps generalize the exponential map and provide a convenient tool for performing numerical integration on manifolds. In nonholonomic mechanics, the constraints restrict the dynamics to a nonintegrable distribution rather than the entire tangent bundle. Using the Hamel formulation, the equations of motion can be expressed in local coordinates adapted to this constraint distribution. We then specialize the framework to the case of Lie groups, where both the dynamics and the constraints exhibit symmetries, allowing a simplified formulation of the numerical scheme. The resulting integrator respects the constraint distribution and enforces the nonholonomic constraints at each discrete time step. The approach is illustrated using the Suslov problem.

AI.tldr: A framework for structure-preserving numerical integrators for nonholonomic mechanical systems on Lie groups using retraction maps.
AI.motivation: Nonholonomic constraints restrict dynamics to nonintegrable distributions, requiring specialized numerical methods that preserve both the mechanical structure and constraints. Traditional methods may fail to respect these constraints properly.
AI.method: Uses retraction maps (generalizing exponential maps) for numerical integration on manifolds. Employs Hamel formulation in coordinates adapted to constraint distribution. Specializes to Lie groups where symmetries simplify the formulation, ensuring constraints are enforced at each discrete time step.
AI.result: Develops a general framework for constructing structure-preserving integrators that respect the constraint distribution and enforce nonholonomic constraints at each time step. The approach is demonstrated using the Suslov problem as an illustrative example.
AI.conclusion: Retraction maps provide an effective tool for constructing numerical integrators for nonholonomic mechanical systems on Lie groups that preserve both the geometric structure and constraints, with the Suslov problem serving as a successful application case.

title: A Numerical PDEs Approach to Evolution Equations in Shape Analysis Based on Regularized Morphoelasticity
comment: Master's thesis, Johns Hopkins University, 2026. 52 pages, 22 figures
summary: This work studies a variational formulation and numerical solution of a regularized morphoelasticity problem of shape evolution. The foundation of our analysis is based on the governing equations of linear elasticity, extended to account for volumetric growth. In the morphoelastic framework, the total deformation is decomposed into an elastic component and a growth component, represented by a growth tensor $G$. While the forward one-step problem -- computing displacement given a growth tensor -- is well-established, a more challenging and relevant question in biological modeling is the inverse problem in a continuous sense. While this problem is fundamentally ill-posed without additional constraints, we will explore parametrized growth models inscribed within an optimal control problem inspired by the Large Deformation Diffeomorphic Metric Mapping (LDDMM) framework. By treating the growth process as a path within a shape space, we can define a physically meaningful metric and seek the most plausible, energy-efficient trajectory between configurations. In the construction, a high-order regularization term is introduced. This elevates the governing equations to a high-order elliptic system, ensuring the existence of a smooth solution. This dissertation focuses on the issue of solving this equation efficiently, as this is a key requirement for the feasibility of the overall approach. This will be achieved with the help of finite element solvers, notably from the FEniCSx library in Python. Also, we implement a Mixed Finite Element Method, which decomposes the problem into a system of coupled second-order equations as a treatment of these high-order systems that have significant computational challenges.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a variational framework and numerical methods for solving regularized morphoelasticity problems, focusing on inverse shape evolution between biological configurations using optimal control and LDDMM-inspired approaches.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenging inverse problem in morphoelasticity for biological modeling - determining growth processes that transform one shape into another, which is fundamentally ill-posed without regularization constraints.
AI.method: The method combines morphoelasticity (decomposing deformation into elastic and growth components) with optimal control inspired by LDDMM framework. It introduces high-order regularization to ensure smooth solutions and uses finite element methods (FEniCSx) with Mixed Finite Element Method to handle computational challenges of high-order elliptic systems.
AI.result: The paper develops a complete variational formulation for regularized morphoelasticity problems, establishes existence of smooth solutions through high-order regularization, and proposes efficient numerical solution strategies using finite element methods and mixed formulations.
AI.conclusion: The dissertation provides a mathematically rigorous and computationally feasible framework for solving inverse morphoelasticity problems, enabling the reconstruction of plausible, energy-efficient growth trajectories between biological configurations through efficient numerical implementation.

title: Adaptive LSQR Preconditioning from One Small Sketch
summary: We propose APLICUR, an adaptive preconditioning framework for large-scale linear least-squares (LLS) problems. Using a single small sketch computed once at initialization, APLICUR incrementally refines a CUR-based preconditioner throughout the Krylov solve, interleaving preconditioning with iteration. This enables early convergence without the need to construct a costly high-quality preconditioner upfront. With a modest sketch dimension (typically 5 - 250), largely independent of both the problem size and numerical rank, APLICUR achieves convergence guarantees that are likewise independent of the sketch size. The method is applicable to general matrices without structural assumptions (e.g. need not be heavily overdetermined) and is well suited to large, sparse, or numerically low-rank problems. We conduct extensive numerical studies to examine the behavior of the proposed framework and guide the effective algorithmic design choices. Across a range of test problems, \mainalg{} achieves competitive or improved time-to-accuracy performance compared with established randomized preconditioners, including Blendenpik and Nyström PCG, while maintaining low setup cost and robustness across problem regimes.

AI.tldr: APLICUR: Adaptive preconditioning framework for large-scale linear least-squares using incremental CUR-based refinement with single small sketch, enabling early convergence without costly upfront preconditioner construction.
AI.motivation: Large-scale linear least-squares problems require efficient preconditioning, but constructing high-quality preconditioners upfront is computationally expensive. Existing methods often need structural assumptions or large sketch sizes.
AI.method: Uses single small sketch computed once at initialization, then incrementally refines CUR-based preconditioner throughout Krylov solve, interleaving preconditioning with iteration. Sketch dimension is modest (5-250) and independent of problem size/rank.
AI.result: Achieves convergence guarantees independent of sketch size, competitive or improved time-to-accuracy compared to established methods (Blendenpik, Nyström PCG), with low setup cost and robustness across problem regimes.
AI.conclusion: APLICUR provides efficient adaptive preconditioning for general large-scale LLS problems without structural assumptions, suitable for sparse or low-rank matrices, balancing early convergence with low computational overhead.

title: Learned Dictionaries with Total Variation and Non-Negativity for Single-Cell Microscopy: Convergence Theory and Deterministic Multi-Channel Cell Feature Unification
summary: We introduce a variational dictionary-based learning algorithm with hybrid penalization for single-cell microscopy signals. The cost functional couples a least-squares data fidelity term with total-variation (TV) regularization and a non-negativity constraint, promoting edge-preserving, physically meaningful reconstructions under heterogeneous backgrounds and imaging artifacts. We formulate the learning task with an explicit unitary (orthonormal) constraint on the dictionary operator, ensuring well-conditioned representations and predictable numerical behavior. The resulting optimization problem is solved by an alternating proximal-gradient scheme that combines smooth updates with closed-form proximal steps for non-smooth penalties. We prove that the PDHG iterates converge to the regularized minimizer under an explicit step-size condition ($τσ< 1/8$), and that when the true solution satisfies a variational source condition (VSC), the regularized solution converges to the true solution at the optimal $O(δ)$ rate under a noise-proportional regularization parameter choice $λ\propto δ$. Beyond reconstruction, we address the problem of multi-channel cell feature unification: given five imaging channels of the BSCCM dataset (DPC Left, Right, Top, Bottom, and Brightfield), we propose a \emph{deterministic} approach to synthesize a unified single-cell representation. Rather than probabilistic latent encodings, we pursue a joint dictionary learning framework in which all five channels share a common dictionary, and the sparse codes across channels are combined to form a channel-agnostic cell descriptor. This deterministic unification strategy is mathematically transparent, reproducible, and directly compatible with the clinical requirement that AI systems for diagnostics must be interpretable and auditable.

AI.tldr: A variational dictionary learning method for single-cell microscopy with TV regularization and non-negativity constraints, plus deterministic multi-channel feature unification using shared dictionaries.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in single-cell microscopy reconstruction under heterogeneous backgrounds and imaging artifacts, and to create interpretable, deterministic multi-channel cell representations for clinical diagnostics.
AI.method: Hybrid penalization combining least-squares data fidelity with TV regularization and non-negativity constraints; explicit unitary constraint on dictionary operator; alternating proximal-gradient optimization with proven convergence; deterministic multi-channel unification via shared dictionary learning.
AI.result: Proven convergence to regularized minimizer with explicit step-size condition (τσ<1/8); optimal O(δ) convergence rate under variational source condition; deterministic unified cell descriptors from five imaging channels.
AI.conclusion: The method provides mathematically transparent, reproducible, and interpretable single-cell reconstructions and multi-channel feature unification suitable for clinical diagnostic AI systems requiring auditability.

title: CVT Archives and Chemical Embedding Measures for Multi-Objective Quality Diversity in Molecular Design
summary: Nonlinear optical (NLO) materials are essential for photonic technologies, yet discovering optimal NLO molecules requires balancing multiple competing objectives across vast chemical spaces. Previous work showed that Multi-Objective MAP-Elites (MOME) with grid-based archives discovers diverse, high-quality molecules for electro-optic applications. However, uniform grid partitioning wastes archive capacity on chemically infeasible regions while undersampling high-density areas. We apply MOME with Centroidal Voronoi Tessellation (CVT) archives whose cells are defined by learned embeddings from ChemBERTa-2 Multi-Task Regression reduced via UMAP, capturing chemical similarity beyond simple structural features. We investigate a four-objective NLO molecular design problem: maximizing the $β/ γ$ hyperpolarizability ratio, constraining HOMO-LUMO gap and linear polarizability to target ranges, and minimizing energy per atom. Our results demonstrate that embedding-based measures in CVT archives yield significantly higher median global hypervolume and multi-objective quality diversity scores, while filling nearly all native archive niches.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a CVT-based archive approach using learned chemical embeddings for multi-objective molecular design of NLO materials, achieving better performance than uniform grid archives.
AI.motivation: Traditional uniform grid partitioning in multi-objective molecular design wastes archive capacity on chemically infeasible regions while undersampling high-density areas, limiting discovery of optimal NLO molecules.
AI.method: Applied Multi-Objective MAP-Elites (MOME) with Centroidal Voronoi Tessellation (CVT) archives using learned embeddings from ChemBERTa-2 Multi-Task Regression reduced via UMAP to capture chemical similarity beyond simple structural features.
AI.result: Embedding-based CVT archives yield significantly higher median global hypervolume and multi-objective quality diversity scores while filling nearly all native archive niches compared to uniform grid approaches.
AI.conclusion: Using learned chemical embeddings with CVT archives improves multi-objective molecular design for NLO materials by better capturing chemical similarity and optimizing archive space utilization.

title: Ion Weibel Instability in the hybrid framework: the optimal resolution
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to Physics of Plasmas
summary: The study of collisionless shocks and their role in cosmic-ray acceleration has gained increasing importance through both observations and simulations. Accurately modeling the shock transition region, where particle injection occurs, requires a proper description of the microinstabilities governing its structure. In high-Mach-number shocks, such as those associated with supernova remnants, the ion Weibel instability is believed to provide the dominant dissipation mechanism. In this work, we investigate the ion Weibel instability driven by counterstreaming beams in the presence of an external perpendicular magnetic field. We employ hybrid simulations, in which ions are treated kinetically while electrons are modeled as a charge-neutralizing fluid. Although hybrid models are widely employed to study collisionless shocks, the resolution requirements needed to accurately capture ion-scale instabilities remain poorly understood. We address this issue by developing a linear theory of the ion Weibel instability tailored to the massless electron assumption of hybrid models and validating it with one- and two-dimensional simulations over a wide range of Alfvénic Mach numbers. We show that hybrid simulations can reliably reproduce the growth, saturation, and polarization of Weibel-generated magnetic fields in weakly magnetized regimes, provided that the relevant ion-scale modes are properly resolved. From the scaling of the dominant mode, we derive a minimum spatial resolution required as a function of Alfvénic Mach number. We also demonstrate that excessive resolution introduces unphysical small-scale whistler modes inherent to the massless-electron approximation. We validate the analysis by comparing the results with full particle-in-cell simulations. Together, these results provide practical guidance for hybrid simulations of collisionless shocks and beam-driven plasma systems.

AI.tldr: Hybrid simulations can accurately model ion Weibel instability in collisionless shocks if proper resolution is used, with guidelines provided for resolution requirements and limitations.
AI.motivation: To understand resolution requirements for hybrid simulations of collisionless shocks, particularly for accurately capturing ion Weibel instability which dominates dissipation in high-Mach-number shocks like supernova remnants.
AI.method: Develop linear theory of ion Weibel instability tailored to hybrid models' massless electron assumption, validate with 1D/2D hybrid simulations across Alfvénic Mach numbers, and compare with full particle-in-cell simulations.
AI.result: Hybrid simulations reliably reproduce Weibel instability growth, saturation, and polarization in weakly magnetized regimes when ion-scale modes are properly resolved. Derived minimum spatial resolution as function of Alfvénic Mach number, and identified that excessive resolution introduces unphysical whistler modes.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical guidance for hybrid simulations of collisionless shocks and beam-driven plasma systems, establishing resolution requirements and limitations for accurate modeling of ion Weibel instability.

title: Global boundary stabilization of 1d systems of scalar conservation laws
summary: We study a system of several one-dimensional scalar conservation laws coupled through boundary feedback conditions that combine physical boundary constraints with static feedback control laws. Our first contribution establishes the well-posedness of the system in the space of $L^{\infty}$ entropy solutions. Our second contribution provides a set of sufficient dissipative conditions on the boundary coupling that ensure global exponential stability in the $L^1$ and $L^\infty$ norms.

AI.tldr: Study of 1D scalar conservation laws with boundary feedback control, establishing well-posedness of entropy solutions and sufficient dissipative conditions for global exponential stability.
AI.motivation: To analyze systems of conservation laws with boundary feedback control, which combine physical boundary constraints with control laws, addressing both well-posedness and stability properties.
AI.method: Study a system of several 1D scalar conservation laws coupled through boundary feedback conditions. Establish well-posedness in L∞ entropy solutions space and provide sufficient dissipative conditions on boundary coupling.
AI.result: First result: Well-posedness established for the system in L∞ entropy solutions space. Second result: Sufficient dissipative conditions on boundary coupling ensure global exponential stability in both L¹ and L∞ norms.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes both well-posedness and stability results for systems of conservation laws with boundary feedback control, providing mathematical foundations for such coupled systems.

title: An adaptive radial basis function approach for efficiently solving multidimensional spatiotemporal integrodifferential equations
summary: In this work, we propose an adaptive radial basis function (RBF) approach for the efficient solution of multidimensional spatiotemporal integrodifferential equations. Our approach can automatically adjust the shape of RBFs and provide an easy-to-implement and mesh-free approach for solving spatiotemporal equations. Specifically, we analyze how the proposed method mitigates the curse of dimensionality by adaptively adjusting the scales and centers of the radial basis functions when the solution is spatially anisotropic. Through a range of numerical examples, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach for solving multidimensional spatiotemporal integrodifferential equations.

AI.tldr: Proposes adaptive RBF method for solving multidimensional spatiotemporal integrodifferential equations with automatic shape adjustment and mesh-free implementation.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient solution method for multidimensional spatiotemporal integrodifferential equations that addresses the curse of dimensionality and provides a mesh-free approach.
AI.method: Adaptive radial basis function (RBF) approach that automatically adjusts RBF shapes, scales, and centers to handle spatial anisotropy in solutions.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness through numerical examples, showing the method mitigates curse of dimensionality for multidimensional spatiotemporal equations.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive RBF approach provides an efficient, mesh-free solution method for multidimensional spatiotemporal integrodifferential equations with automatic shape adjustment capabilities.

title: Efficient High-order Mass-conserving and Energy-balancing Schemes for Schrödinger-Poisson Equations
summary: We study relaxation-based approaches for conserving mass and energy in the numerical solution of Schrödinger-Poisson (SP) type systems. Relaxation-based methods offer a general approach that can be applied as post-time step processing to achieve conservation with any time-stepping scheme. Here we study two types of relaxation techniques applied to implicit-explicit Runge-Kutta schemes, with Fourier collocation in space. We also study SP equations with time-varying coefficients (which appear naturally in cosmology) where energy is not conserved but satisfies a balance equation. We show that the fully-discrete system conserves both mass and energy (or satisfies the balance equation in case of time-varying coefficients), up to rounding errors. The effectiveness of these methods is demonstrated via numerical examples, including a three-dimensional cosmological simulation.

AI.tldr: Relaxation-based methods conserve mass and energy in Schrödinger-Poisson systems, working as post-processing for any time-stepping scheme, demonstrated with IMEX-RK and Fourier collocation.
AI.motivation: Need to conserve mass and energy in numerical solutions of Schrödinger-Poisson systems, especially for applications like cosmology where time-varying coefficients appear and energy satisfies balance equations rather than strict conservation.
AI.method: Two types of relaxation techniques applied to implicit-explicit Runge-Kutta (IMEX-RK) schemes with Fourier collocation in space, used as post-time step processing to enforce conservation properties.
AI.result: Fully-discrete system conserves both mass and energy (or satisfies balance equation for time-varying coefficients) up to rounding errors, demonstrated via numerical examples including 3D cosmological simulation.
AI.conclusion: Relaxation-based approaches provide effective general framework for conserving mass and energy in SP systems, applicable to various time-stepping schemes and handling time-varying coefficient cases common in cosmology.

title: Evolution of SPI-induced disruptions in ASDEX Upgrade
summary: Disruptions are a major concern for future fusion reactors based on the tokamak principle. To ensure machine protection, the thermal loads and vessel forces that arise during disruptions have to be mitigated reliably. For the ITER disruption mitigation system (DMS), the shattered pellet injection (SPI) technology has been selected. It can provide a prompt delivery of the injection material into the plasma core, with the mitigation efficiency depending on fragment size and velocity. A highly flexible SPI system was built and installed at the tokamak ASDEX Upgrade (AUG) to aid the finalization process of the ITER DMS and provide crucial input for modeling. The SPI-induced disruptions in the 2022 AUG experiments follow a typical chain of events, which are discussed in this paper: The first light, main fragment arrival, plasma movement event, MARFE, thermal quench/plasma current spike, current quench, and vertical displacement event phase. Depending on the injection parameters, these phases may vary significantly or some might not be present at all. In this paper, we will focus on the characterization of these disruption phases and figures of merit for the mitigation efficiency, depending on the SPI configuration. With increasing amount of assimilated neon in the plasma - primarily influenced by the neon content in the pellet but also the shattering parameters - the disruptions exhibit different behaviors. This disruption evolution seems to be a continuous process, with the most prominent feature being the changing disruption time scales and plasma current time trace shape during the CQ from convex (poorly or unmitigated) $\rightarrow$ concave (well mitigated/radiation dominated). Depending on the injection, pre-TQ durations between 15 - 0.5 ms and early CQ durations ($Δ\textrm{t}_\textrm{CQ}^{100 \rightarrow 80}$) between 13.3 - 8.2 ms had been achieved at AUG.

AI.tldr: SPI-induced disruptions at ASDEX Upgrade show varying disruption phases and mitigation efficiency depending on neon content and shattering parameters, with disruption time scales changing from convex to concave current quench shapes.
AI.motivation: To study disruption mitigation for ITER using shattered pellet injection (SPI) technology, as disruptions pose major concerns for future fusion reactors and machine protection requires reliable mitigation of thermal loads and vessel forces.
AI.method: Built and installed a highly flexible SPI system at ASDEX Upgrade tokamak to induce disruptions with varying injection parameters (neon content, shattering parameters), then characterized disruption phases and mitigation efficiency.
AI.result: SPI-induced disruptions follow a typical chain of events with varying phases depending on injection parameters. With increasing assimilated neon, disruptions show changing time scales and plasma current trace shapes during current quench from convex (poorly mitigated) to concave (well mitigated/radiation dominated). Achieved pre-TQ durations of 15-0.5 ms and early CQ durations of 13.3-8.2 ms.
AI.conclusion: SPI disruption evolution is a continuous process where mitigation efficiency depends on material assimilation, with neon content and shattering parameters significantly affecting disruption behavior and time scales, providing crucial input for ITER DMS modeling.

title: Blow-up rates and sets for a quasilinear diffusion equation with weighted source
summary: Blow-up rates are established for general solutions to the quasilinear diffusion equation $$ \partial_tu=Δu^m+|x|^σu^p, \quad (x,t)\in\mathbb{R}^N\times(0,T), $$ in the range of exponents $1<p<m$, $σ>0$. More precisely, if we consider a compactly supported solution $u(x,t)$ with blow-up time $T=T(u)\in(0,\infty)$, we derive the blow-up rate $$ C_1(T-t)^{-α}\leq \|u(x,t)\|_{\infty}\leq C_2(T-t)^{-α}, \quad t\in(0,T), $$ for some positive constants $C_1$, $C_2$, and the upper rate of expansion of the support $$ \sup\{|x|:u(x,t)>0\}\leq C_0(T-t)^{-β}, \quad t\in(0,T), $$ for some constant $C_0>0$, where $$ α=\frac{σ+2}{L}, \quad β=\frac{m-p}{L}, \quad L=σ(m-1)+2(p-1). $$ We also analyze the blow-up sets of solutions $u$, showing, under a suitable condition, that either $B(u)=\mathbb{R}^N$ or blow-up takes place only as $|x|\to\infty$.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes blow-up rates and support expansion rates for solutions to a quasilinear diffusion equation with source term, focusing on the exponent range 1<p<m, σ>0.
AI.motivation: To understand the blow-up behavior (finite-time singularity formation) of solutions to quasilinear diffusion equations with spatially dependent source terms, which are important in modeling various physical phenomena like combustion and population dynamics.
AI.method: Analysis of the quasilinear diffusion equation ∂_t u = Δu^m + |x|^σ u^p using mathematical techniques to derive precise blow-up rates and support expansion bounds for compactly supported solutions.
AI.result: Derived exact blow-up rate C_1(T-t)^{-α} ≤ ||u(x,t)||_∞ ≤ C_2(T-t)^{-α} and support expansion rate sup{|x|: u(x,t)>0} ≤ C_0(T-t)^{-β}, with explicit formulas for α, β, L in terms of exponents m, p, σ.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides complete characterization of blow-up rates and support expansion for the quasilinear diffusion equation, showing either blow-up occurs everywhere in space or only at spatial infinity under suitable conditions.

title: Multi-Scale Kinetic Simulation: Asymptotic Preserving IMEX-BDF-DG Schemes with Three Implicit-Explicit Partitionings
summary: Kinetic transport models are mesoscopic mathematical descriptions of the transport of particles as well as their interactions with the background media or among themselves, and they have wide applications in many areas of mathematical physics such as nuclear and biomedical engineering, rarefied gas dynamics, and plasma physics. They are often multi-scale, with different characteristics (e.g. hyperbolic, diffusive) depending on the material properties. As our continuing effort to design and analyze numerical methods for accurate and robust simulation of the multi-scale kinetic transport models, in this work, we consider a linear kinetic transport model, a simplified radiative transfer equation, in a diffusive scaling, and propose and analyze three families of asymptotic preserving (AP) methods. Numerical methods with the AP property, that is to preserve the asymptotic behavior of the models at the discrete level on under-resolved meshes, can work uniformly well to simulate multi-scale models across a wide range of scales. The proposed methods start from the micro-macro decomposition of the model, and involve discontinuous Galerkin (DG) methods in space, the discrete ordinates method (i.e. $S_N$ method) in velocity, and implicit-explicit (IMEX) BDF methods in time, with three different IMEX partitionings. A systematic study, both analytically and computationally, is presented regarding their difference in stability, accuracy, computational complexity and AP property. These methods, with multi-step time integrators, are also compared in terms of their accuracy and efficiency with the ones that only differ in using certain IMEX Runge-Kutta methods in time. Together with our previous developments, the present work further contributes to high order DG AP methods for multi-scale kinetic simulation, especially by utilizing the structure of the micro-macro decomposition of the models.

AI.tldr: Three families of asymptotic preserving (AP) methods are proposed for linear kinetic transport models using DG in space, S_N in velocity, and IMEX-BDF in time with different partitionings, analyzed for stability, accuracy, and AP properties.
AI.motivation: To develop accurate and robust numerical methods for multi-scale kinetic transport models that can work uniformly well across different scales, preserving asymptotic behavior on under-resolved meshes.
AI.method: Micro-macro decomposition approach with discontinuous Galerkin (DG) methods in space, discrete ordinates (S_N) method in velocity, and implicit-explicit (IMEX) BDF methods in time with three different partitionings.
AI.result: Systematic analytical and computational study of stability, accuracy, computational complexity, and AP properties; comparison with IMEX Runge-Kutta methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Contributes to high-order DG AP methods for multi-scale kinetic simulation by leveraging micro-macro decomposition structure, advancing numerical methods for kinetic transport models.

title: A deep learning framework for jointly solving transient Fokker-Planck equations with arbitrary parameters and initial distributions
summary: Efficiently solving the Fokker-Planck equation (FPE) is central to analyzing complex parameterized stochastic systems. However, current numerical methods lack parallel computation capabilities across varying conditions, severely limiting comprehensive parameter exploration and transient analysis. This paper introduces a deep learning-based pseudo-analytical probability solution (PAPS) that, via a single training process, simultaneously resolves transient FPE solutions for arbitrary multi-modal initial distributions, system parameters, and time points. The core idea is to unify initial, transient, and stationary distributions via Gaussian mixture distributions (GMDs) and develop a constraint-preserving autoencoder that bijectively maps constrained GMD parameters to unconstrained, low-dimensional latent representations. In this representation space, the panoramic transient dynamics across varying initial conditions and system parameters can be modeled by a single evolution network. Extensive experiments on paradigmatic systems demonstrate that the proposed PAPS maintains high accuracy while achieving inference speeds four orders of magnitude faster than GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo simulations. This efficiency leap enables previously intractable real-time parameter sweeps and systematic investigations of stochastic bifurcations. By decoupling representation learning from physics-informed transient dynamics, our work establishes a scalable paradigm for probabilistic modeling of multi-dimensional, parameterized stochastic systems.

AI.tldr: Deep learning-based pseudo-analytical solution for Fokker-Planck equation that simultaneously resolves transient solutions for arbitrary multi-modal initial distributions, system parameters, and time points via a single training process.
AI.motivation: Current numerical methods for solving Fokker-Planck equations lack parallel computation capabilities across varying conditions, severely limiting comprehensive parameter exploration and transient analysis of complex stochastic systems.
AI.method: Unifies initial, transient, and stationary distributions via Gaussian mixture distributions (GMDs) and develops a constraint-preserving autoencoder that bijectively maps constrained GMD parameters to unconstrained, low-dimensional latent representations. In this representation space, panoramic transient dynamics across varying initial conditions and system parameters are modeled by a single evolution network.
AI.result: The proposed PAPS maintains high accuracy while achieving inference speeds four orders of magnitude faster than GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo simulations, enabling previously intractable real-time parameter sweeps and systematic investigations of stochastic bifurcations.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling representation learning from physics-informed transient dynamics, this work establishes a scalable paradigm for probabilistic modeling of multi-dimensional, parameterized stochastic systems.

title: Development of a 3D-CNN-based Prediction Model for Migration Barriers in Plasma-Wall Interactions
summary: Understanding the long-term transport of hydrogen isotopes in plasma-facing materials, such as tungsten, is critical for the steady-state operation of magnetic confinement fusion reactors. However, dynamically updating the transition parameters for kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulations as the atomic structure evolves under continuous plasma irradiation remains a severe computational bottleneck. Conventionally, calculating these migration barriers requires the iterative and computationally expensive Nudged Elastic Band (NEB) method. To overcome this limitation, this article presents a highly efficient surrogate model for predicting migration barriers using a three-dimensional Convolutional Neural Network (3D-CNN), establishing the final component necessary to realize on-the-fly molecular dynamics (MD) and kMC hybrid simulations. The proposed deep learning model takes a two-channel volumetric input, the local three-dimensional potential energy distribution and the voxelized spatial coordinates of the initial and final trapping sites, to directly output the migration barrier as a scalar value. Trained on a comprehensive dataset of tungsten-hydrogen configurations evaluated using the Embedded Atom Method (EAM) potential, the model demonstrated robust predictive accuracy, achieving a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.124 eV and a high coefficient of determination of 0.890. Furthermore, utilizing GPU acceleration, the inference time is reduced to approximately 2.7 milliseconds per barrier, achieving a speed-up ratio of over 23,000 compared to conventional NEB calculations. This extraordinary acceleration effectively resolves the computational barrier of transition rate evaluations, paving the way for large-scale, dynamic modeling of plasma-wall interactions.

AI.tldr: A 3D-CNN surrogate model predicts hydrogen migration barriers in tungsten 23,000× faster than conventional NEB calculations, enabling on-the-fly hybrid MD/kMC simulations for fusion reactor plasma-wall interactions.
AI.motivation: The computational bottleneck of dynamically updating transition parameters (migration barriers) for kinetic Monte Carlo simulations as atomic structure evolves under plasma irradiation in fusion reactors. Conventional Nudged Elastic Band method is too expensive for real-time updates.
AI.method: A three-dimensional Convolutional Neural Network (3D-CNN) takes two-channel volumetric input: local 3D potential energy distribution and voxelized spatial coordinates of initial/final trapping sites, directly outputting migration barrier as scalar. Trained on tungsten-hydrogen configurations using Embedded Atom Method potential.
AI.result: Model achieved MAE of 0.124 eV and R² of 0.890. GPU acceleration reduced inference time to ~2.7 ms per barrier, achieving 23,000× speed-up compared to conventional NEB calculations.
AI.conclusion: The deep learning surrogate model effectively resolves computational barrier for transition rate evaluations, enabling large-scale dynamic modeling of plasma-wall interactions and paving way for on-the-fly MD/kMC hybrid simulations in fusion reactor research.

title: The best constant in the G-N inequality for the mixed local and Nonlocal Laplacian
summary: In this paper, we establish the best constant in the G-N inequality for the mixed local and nonlocal Laplacian. In our problem, classical methods cannot apply directly since regularity results for the operator under study seem to be highly challenging. We build an innovative method that not only enabled us to prove that the optimizer of the best constant is the ground state solution of the equation, but also to establish the best constant.

AI.tldr: Established optimal constant for Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequality with mixed local-nonlocal Laplacian using novel method to overcome regularity challenges.
AI.motivation: Classical methods fail for mixed local-nonlocal Laplacian due to challenging regularity results, requiring new approach to determine best constant in G-N inequality.
AI.method: Developed innovative method that proves optimizer is ground state solution of the equation, enabling determination of best constant despite regularity challenges.
AI.result: Successfully established the best constant in the Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequality for mixed local and nonlocal Laplacian operator.
AI.conclusion: Novel method overcomes classical limitations, proving ground state solution optimizes inequality and determining optimal constant for mixed operator.

title: Higher-Order Multiscale Computational Method for Multi-Continuum Problems in Highly Heterogeneous Media
summary: This paper presents a high-accuracy higher-order multiscale method for solving multi-continuum problems in in highly heterogeneous media. First, microscopic unit cell functions are defined, leading to the derivation of macroscopic homogenized equations and formulas for calculating effective parameters, which yield a higher-order multi-scale (HOMS) asymptotic solution. Subsequently, the pointwise approximation properties of this solution to the original equations are analyzed, and its convergence rate in the integral norm is rigorously established under certain assumptions. Furthermore, a multiscale numerical algorithm is developed by integrating the finite element method (FEM), finite difference method, and interpolation technique. Finally, numerical experiments demonstrate the high accuracy, efficiency, and stability of the proposed HOMS numerical algorithm.

AI.tldr: A high-order multiscale method for multi-continuum problems in heterogeneous media, combining asymptotic analysis with numerical algorithms for accurate solutions.
AI.motivation: To develop accurate computational methods for multi-continuum problems in highly heterogeneous media, which are challenging due to complex microstructures requiring efficient multiscale approaches.
AI.method: Define microscopic unit cell functions, derive macroscopic homogenized equations and effective parameters, analyze approximation properties, then develop numerical algorithm combining FEM, finite difference, and interpolation techniques.
AI.result: Established convergence rates for the asymptotic solution, developed a stable numerical algorithm, and demonstrated high accuracy and efficiency through numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed higher-order multiscale method provides an accurate, efficient, and stable approach for solving multi-continuum problems in heterogeneous media, validated by both theoretical analysis and numerical experiments.

title: Sparse Autoencoders as a Steering Basis for Phase Synchronization in Graph-Based CFD Surrogates
summary: Graph-based surrogate models provide fast alternatives to high-fidelity CFD solvers, but their opaque latent spaces and limited controllability restrict use in safety-critical settings. A key failure mode in oscillatory flows is phase drift, where predictions remain qualitatively correct but gradually lose temporal alignment with observations, limiting use in digital twins and closed-loop control. Correcting this through retraining is expensive and impractical during deployment. We ask whether phase drift can instead be corrected post hoc by manipulating the latent space of a frozen surrogate. We propose a phase-steering framework for pretrained graph-based CFD models that combines the right representation with the right intervention mechanism. To obtain disentangled representation for effective steering, we use sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on frozen MeshGraphNet embeddings. To steer dynamics, we move beyond static per-feature interventions such as scaling or clamping, and introduce a temporally coherent, phase-aware method. Specifically, we identify oscillatory feature pairs with Hilbert analysis, project spatial fields into low-rank temporal coefficients via SVD, and apply smooth time-varying rotations to advance or delay periodic modes while preserving amplitude-phase structure. Using a representation-agnostic setup, we compare SAE-based steering with PCA and raw embedding spaces under the same intervention pipeline. Results show that sparse, disentangled representations outperform dense or entangled ones, while static interventions fail in this dynamical setting. Overall, this work shows that latent-space steering can be extended from semantic domains to time-dependent physical systems when interventions respect the underlying dynamics, and that the same sparse features used for interpretability can also serve as physically meaningful control axes.

AI.tldr: Phase-steering framework corrects phase drift in frozen graph-based CFD surrogates using sparse autoencoders and time-varying rotations on oscillatory features, enabling post-hoc correction without retraining.
AI.motivation: Graph-based surrogate models for CFD suffer from phase drift in oscillatory flows, where predictions lose temporal alignment with observations. This limits use in safety-critical applications like digital twins and closed-loop control. Retraining to correct drift is expensive and impractical during deployment.
AI.method: Propose phase-steering framework: 1) Use sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on frozen MeshGraphNet embeddings to obtain disentangled representations; 2) Identify oscillatory feature pairs via Hilbert analysis; 3) Project spatial fields into low-rank temporal coefficients using SVD; 4) Apply smooth time-varying rotations to advance/delay periodic modes while preserving amplitude-phase structure.
AI.result: Sparse, disentangled representations (SAEs) outperform dense or entangled ones (PCA, raw embeddings) under the same intervention pipeline. Static interventions fail in dynamical settings, while the proposed temporally coherent, phase-aware method successfully corrects phase drift.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space steering can be extended from semantic domains to time-dependent physical systems when interventions respect underlying dynamics. Sparse features used for interpretability can also serve as physically meaningful control axes for correcting phase drift in frozen surrogate models.

title: Modeling complex plasma instabilities in space plasmas - Three-component electron formalism of heat-flux instabilities
comment: 12 pages, 9 figures, accepted for publication in Astronomy & Astrophysics
summary: Despite the fact that electrons observed in situ in space plasmas have three major components-the quasi-thermal core, suprathermal halo, and strahl-the analysis of instabilities triggered by kinetic, velocity-space anisotropies (such as relative drifts and temperature anisotropy) generally considers only two. We demonstrate that realistic modeling with all three components is achievable in the present analysis focusing on heat-flux instabilities. In the absence of particle collisions, these instabilities regulate the heat flux carried mainly by suprathermal electrons. The velocity distributions were modeled according to in situ observations, with a Maxwellian core and Kappa-distributed halo and strahl. We exploited advanced numerical codes capable of solving the linear dispersion and stability properties of plasma systems with Maxwellian and Kappa distributions. The unstable solutions differ significantly from those obtained with simplified two-component models (such as core-strahl or core-beam). The growth rates predict the excitation and interplay of two unstable modes, whistler and/or firehose heat-flux instabilities. The numerical solver 'ALPS' was successfully applied to systems with regularized Kappa distributions, for which analytical derivation of dispersion relations is not straightforward. The two instabilities are triggered by the relative drifts, core-strahl and halo-strahl, and may have new consequences for heat-flux regulation. Particularly important are cases when the core-strahl instability is in competition with the instability driven by the halo-strahl drift, as well as when the two instabilities have the same nature and accumulate. Future studies are motivated to confirm these predictions in quasilinear theories and numerical simulations.

AI.tldr: Three-component electron model (core, halo, strahl) reveals new heat-flux instabilities beyond simplified two-component models, with whistler and firehose modes driven by core-strahl and halo-strahl drifts.
AI.motivation: Previous analyses of kinetic instabilities in space plasmas used simplified two-component electron models, ignoring the realistic three-component structure (core, halo, strahl) observed in situ. This oversimplification may miss important physics in heat-flux regulation.
AI.method: Used realistic three-component electron distributions: Maxwellian core + Kappa-distributed halo + Kappa-distributed strahl. Applied advanced numerical codes (including 'ALPS') to solve linear dispersion relations for systems with regularized Kappa distributions where analytical solutions are difficult.
AI.result: Found significantly different unstable solutions compared to two-component models. Identified excitation and interplay of two unstable modes: whistler and firehose heat-flux instabilities. These are triggered by core-strahl and halo-strahl relative drifts, with important cases where the two instabilities compete or accumulate.
AI.conclusion: Realistic three-component modeling reveals new heat-flux regulation mechanisms through competing instabilities. The 'ALPS' solver successfully handles complex Kappa distributions. Future work should confirm predictions via quasilinear theories and numerical simulations.

title: The blow-up rate for a log non-scaling invariant semilinear wave equation in the conformal regime
summary: We consider the blow-up behavior of solutions to the semilinear wave equation \[ \partial_t^2 u - Δu = |u|^{p-1}u \ln^a(u^2+2), \qquad (x,t)\in \mathbb{R}^n \times [0,T), \] in the conformal case \( p = p_c = 1 + \frac{4}{n-1} \). Previous results in \cite{HZjmaa2020, HZ2022} show that for \( a \in \mathbb{R} \), solutions in the subconformal regime \( p < p_c \) blow up with a Type~I rate at any non-characteristic point. The objective of this work is to extend this blow-up rate to the conformal regime under the assumption \( a < 0 \). We establish an a priori upper bound for any blow-up solution and construct a Lyapunov functional in similarity variables. The resulting functional exhibits only weak dissipation, which necessitates delicate energy arguments to obtain the sharp blow-up rate in the conformal case. To the best of our knowledge, this provides the first result for the blow-up rate in a critical framework for an evolution problem where the scaling symmetry is broken.

AI.tldr: Analysis of blow-up behavior for semilinear wave equation with logarithmic nonlinearity in conformal case p=p_c, showing Type I blow-up rate when a<0.
AI.motivation: Extend previous results on blow-up rates from subconformal regime (p<p_c) to the conformal regime (p=p_c) for semilinear wave equations with logarithmic nonlinearity, particularly when a<0. Address critical case where scaling symmetry is broken.
AI.method: Establish a priori upper bound for blow-up solutions, construct Lyapunov functional in similarity variables, use delicate energy arguments to handle weak dissipation in the functional, and analyze blow-up behavior at non-characteristic points.
AI.result: Prove Type I blow-up rate for solutions in conformal regime when a<0, providing first result for blow-up rate in critical framework where scaling symmetry is broken.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extend blow-up rate results to conformal case with logarithmic nonlinearity, demonstrating Type I blow-up behavior when a<0 despite broken scaling symmetry in critical framework.

title: Mathematical analysis and symmetric fractional-order reduction method for diffusion-wave equations
summary: In this work, our aim is to introduce a symmetric fractional-order reduction (SFOR) method to develop numerical algorithms on nonuniform temporal meshes for fractional wave equations under lower regularity assumptions. The $L$-type methods--including $L1$ and $L2$-$1_σ$ schemes--are specifically designed for diffusion-wave equations, and we propose novel optimal parameter selections tailored to nonuniform meshes. Finally, several numerical experiments are conducted to validate the efficiency and accuracy of the algorithms.

AI.tldr: Proposes symmetric fractional-order reduction method for fractional wave equations on nonuniform meshes with optimal parameter selection for L-type schemes.
AI.motivation: To develop numerical algorithms for fractional wave equations on nonuniform temporal meshes under lower regularity assumptions, addressing limitations of existing methods.
AI.method: Introduces symmetric fractional-order reduction (SFOR) method, proposes novel optimal parameter selections for L-type methods (L1 and L2-1σ schemes) tailored to nonuniform meshes.
AI.result: Numerical experiments validate the efficiency and accuracy of the proposed algorithms for fractional wave equations.
AI.conclusion: The SFOR method with optimal parameter selection provides effective numerical algorithms for fractional wave equations on nonuniform meshes under low regularity conditions.

title: A Theory-guided Weighted $L^2$ Loss for solving the BGK model via Physics-informed neural networks
summary: While Physics-Informed Neural Networks offer a promising framework for solving partial differential equations, the standard $L^2$ loss formulation is fundamentally insufficient when applied to the Bhatnagar-Gross-Krook (BGK) model. Specifically, simply minimizing the standard loss does not guarantee accurate predictions of the macroscopic moments, causing the approximate solutions to fail in capturing the true physical solution. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a velocity-weighted $L^2$ loss function designed to effectively penalize errors in the high-velocity regions. By establishing a stability estimate for the proposed approach, we shows that minimizing the proposed weighted loss guarantees the convergence of the approximate solution. Also, numerical experiments demonstrate that employing this weighted PINN loss leads to superior accuracy and robustness across various benchmarks compared to the standard approach.

AI.tldr: PINNs with standard L² loss fail for BGK model; proposed velocity-weighted L² loss improves accuracy by penalizing high-velocity errors.
AI.motivation: Standard Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) using L² loss are insufficient for solving the Bhatnagar-Gross-Krook (BGK) model because they don't guarantee accurate predictions of macroscopic moments, causing solutions to fail in capturing true physical behavior.
AI.method: Introduce a velocity-weighted L² loss function that effectively penalizes errors in high-velocity regions, with theoretical stability estimates showing convergence guarantees.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate superior accuracy and robustness across various benchmarks compared to standard PINN approach.
AI.conclusion: Velocity-weighted loss formulation overcomes limitations of standard PINNs for BGK model, ensuring better physical solution capture through targeted error penalization in critical velocity regions.

title: Occurrence of Flat-top Electron Velocity Distributions in Magnetotail Plasma Jets
summary: Non-Maxwellian electron velocity distributions (eVDFs) are ubiquitous in collisionless plasmas. For example, various types of non-Maxwellian eVDFs exist in magnetic reconnection jets in the Earth's magnetotail. At thermal energies, eVDF can be flat-topped due to electron trapping associated with magnetic reconnection. However, the occurrence of such eVDFs in magnetotail reconnection remains largely unconstrained. Here, we statistically investigate flat-top eVDFs in fast plasma jets in the magnetotail using a new method for classifying eVDFs. We show that only $\sim 7\%$ of the eVDFs in the jets are flat-tops. Nevertheless, we find that most jets exhibit flat-top eVDFs, indicating that this signature of parallel acceleration and electron streaming is characteristic of the jets. We find that these flat-top eVDFs are localized within an ion-inertial-length-scale region near the edges of the current sheet and close to the reconnection region. Our results highlight the importance of flat-top eVDFs in non-local thermodynamic equilibrium collisionless plasmas.

AI.tldr: Statistical analysis reveals that only ~7% of electron velocity distributions in magnetotail reconnection jets are flat-tops, but most jets contain them, indicating they're characteristic signatures of parallel acceleration and electron streaming localized near current sheet edges.
AI.motivation: Non-Maxwellian electron velocity distributions are common in collisionless plasmas like Earth's magnetotail reconnection jets, but the occurrence of flat-top eVDFs (associated with electron trapping during reconnection) remains poorly constrained and requires systematic investigation.
AI.method: Used a new classification method to statistically analyze flat-top eVDFs in fast plasma jets in the magnetotail, examining their occurrence rates and spatial distribution relative to current sheets and reconnection regions.
AI.result: Only ~7% of eVDFs in jets are flat-tops, but most jets exhibit them. These flat-top eVDFs are localized within ion-inertial-length-scale regions near current sheet edges and close to reconnection regions.
AI.conclusion: Flat-top eVDFs represent important signatures of parallel acceleration and electron streaming in collisionless plasmas, highlighting their significance in non-local thermodynamic equilibrium processes in magnetotail reconnection.

title: The two-phase Alt-Phillips problem for quasilinear operators
summary: We establish interior regularity, optimal growth, and nondegeneracy estimates for sign-changing minimizers of the $p-$singular or $p-$degenerate quasilinear Alt--Phillips functional throughout the full range of $1<p<+\infty$ and of the nonlinearity power $0<γ<p$. In addition, we obtain local finite perimeter and density estimates, from which we deduce the local $(N-1)$-rectifiability of the reduced and two-phase free boundaries and the local finiteness of their $(N-1)$-dimensional Hausdorff measure.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes regularity, growth, and nondegeneracy estimates for sign-changing minimizers of the p-singular/degenerate Alt-Phillips functional across all p>1 and γ<p, plus free boundary regularity results.
AI.motivation: To understand the regularity properties of minimizers of quasilinear Alt-Phillips functionals with sign-changing solutions, which are important in free boundary problems and nonlinear PDEs.
AI.method: The authors establish interior regularity estimates, optimal growth bounds, and nondegeneracy estimates for sign-changing minimizers. They also prove local finite perimeter and density estimates for the free boundaries.
AI.result: The paper proves interior regularity, optimal growth, and nondegeneracy estimates for all 1<p<∞ and 0<γ<p. It shows local (N-1)-rectifiability of reduced and two-phase free boundaries and local finiteness of their Hausdorff measure.
AI.conclusion: The results provide comprehensive regularity theory for sign-changing minimizers of p-singular/degenerate Alt-Phillips functionals, establishing important properties of their free boundaries including rectifiability and Hausdorff measure finiteness.

title: HDG Methods for the two-dimensional Vector Laplacian
summary: We introduce new hybridizable discontinuous Galerkin (HDG) methods for solving the two-dimensional vector Laplacian equation under three types of boundary conditions: electric, magnetic, and Dirichlet. The method is formulated on a first-order system form of the equations, in which the rotational and divergence of the electric field are introduced as auxiliary variables. We study the well-posedness of the method and prove that, when using piecewise polynomial approximations of degree $k \geq 0$, the error in the $L^2$ norm of the electric field converges at the optimal rate of $k+1$. Additionally, we prove that the $L^2$-errors of the auxiliary variables, the rotational and divergence, converge with order $k + 1/2$. We also show that the methods can be implemented in three different forms, corresponding to three distinct hybridizations based on the choice of the globally coupled unknowns among the numerical traces defined on the mesh skeleton. Finally, we provide numerical tests that not only validate the theoretical convergence rates but also consistently showcase the optimal convergence across all variables.

AI.tldr: New HDG methods for 2D vector Laplacian with optimal convergence rates for electric field and suboptimal rates for auxiliary variables.
AI.motivation: To develop efficient numerical methods for solving the two-dimensional vector Laplacian equation under different boundary conditions (electric, magnetic, Dirichlet) using hybridizable discontinuous Galerkin approaches.
AI.method: Hybridizable discontinuous Galerkin (HDG) methods formulated on a first-order system with rotational and divergence of electric field as auxiliary variables. Three different hybridizations based on choice of globally coupled unknowns among numerical traces on mesh skeleton.
AI.result: Proved well-posedness and optimal convergence rate of k+1 for electric field in L² norm. Suboptimal convergence rate of k+1/2 for auxiliary variables (rotational and divergence). Three implementation forms validated through numerical tests.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HDG methods provide effective numerical solutions for vector Laplacian problems with proven convergence rates and flexible implementation options through different hybridization strategies.

title: Jeffreys Flow: Robust Boltzmann Generators for Rare Event Sampling via Parallel Tempering Distillation
summary: Sampling physical systems with rough energy landscapes is hindered by rare events and metastable trapping. While Boltzmann generators already offer a solution, their reliance on the reverse Kullback--Leibler divergence frequently induces catastrophic mode collapse, missing specific modes in multi-modal distributions. Here, we introduce the Jeffreys Flow, a robust generative framework that mitigates this failure by distilling empirical sampling data from Parallel Tempering trajectories using the symmetric Jeffreys divergence. This formulation effectively balances local target-seeking precision with global modes coverage. We show that minimizing Jeffreys divergence suppresses mode collapse and structurally corrects inherent inaccuracies via distillation of the empirical reference data. We demonstrate the framework's scalability and accuracy on highly non-convex multidimensional benchmarks, including the systematic correction of stochastic gradient biases in Replica Exchange Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics and the massive acceleration of exact importance sampling in Path Integral Monte Carlo for quantum thermal states.

AI.tldr: Jeffreys Flow: A robust generative framework using symmetric Jeffreys divergence to prevent mode collapse in sampling rough energy landscapes, outperforming Boltzmann generators that suffer from catastrophic mode collapse.
AI.motivation: Sampling physical systems with rough energy landscapes faces challenges of rare events and metastable trapping. Boltzmann generators offer a solution but suffer from catastrophic mode collapse when using reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence, missing specific modes in multi-modal distributions.
AI.method: Introduces Jeffreys Flow, a generative framework that distills empirical sampling data from Parallel Tempering trajectories using the symmetric Jeffreys divergence. This formulation balances local target-seeking precision with global mode coverage, suppressing mode collapse and correcting inherent inaccuracies via distillation of empirical reference data.
AI.result: Demonstrates scalability and accuracy on highly non-convex multidimensional benchmarks, including systematic correction of stochastic gradient biases in Replica Exchange Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics and massive acceleration of exact importance sampling in Path Integral Monte Carlo for quantum thermal states.
AI.conclusion: Jeffreys Flow provides a robust solution to mode collapse in generative sampling of complex physical systems, offering improved mode coverage and accuracy compared to existing Boltzmann generator approaches.

title: gyaradax: Local Gyrokinetics JAX Code
summary: Gyrokinetic simulations are essential for understanding and controlling turbulence in fusion plasmas, yet they are oftentimes implemented in legacy codebases, in many cases CPU-bound. These are both hard to maintain and especially incompatible with optimization and ML workflows. gyaradax is a minimal JAX/CUDA solver for local flux-tube gyrokinetics. We base our implementation on GKW (Peeters et al., 2009), but with added native GPU acceleration and automatic differentiation. We validate gyaradax against analytical cases and empirical benchmarks, achieving formal agreement and statistical parity with GKW alongside a substantial speedup. We deliberately and extensively utilized agentic workflows in this project. A key contribution is showing that coding agents, guided by human expertise, structured prompting, and measurable progress through unit testing enabled extremely fast translation of complex Fortran code, and further optimizations. Gyaradax facilitates research at the intersection of ML and plasma physics. We showcase this through practical examples in inverse problems and sensitivity analysis.

AI.tldr: Gyaradax is a JAX/CUDA-based GPU-accelerated gyrokinetic solver with automatic differentiation, enabling ML workflows in plasma physics with significant speedups over legacy Fortran code.
AI.motivation: Legacy gyrokinetic simulation codebases are CPU-bound, hard to maintain, and incompatible with modern optimization and machine learning workflows, creating a need for GPU-accelerated, differentiable implementations.
AI.method: Developed a minimal JAX/CUDA solver based on GKW framework, added native GPU acceleration and automatic differentiation, validated against analytical cases and GKW benchmarks, and extensively used agentic workflows with structured prompting and unit testing for rapid code translation.
AI.result: Achieved formal agreement and statistical parity with GKW benchmarks, substantial speedup over legacy code, and successful demonstration of practical applications in inverse problems and sensitivity analysis at the ML-plasma physics intersection.
AI.conclusion: Gyaradax successfully bridges the gap between legacy plasma physics simulations and modern ML workflows, showing that agentic development approaches can rapidly translate complex Fortran code while enabling new research directions in fusion plasma turbulence.

title: Global existence of weak solutions for Landau-Lifshitz equation with helical derivatives
summary: In this paper, we investigate the chiral boundary value problem for the Landau-Lifshitz equation with helical derivatives. By introducing Sobolev spaces adapted to the helical derivative and establishing energy estimates that are compatible with the chiral boundary condition, we prove the global existence of weak solutions to this problem, both in the presence and in the absence of damping terms.

AI.tldr: Global existence of weak solutions for Landau-Lifshitz equation with helical derivatives and chiral boundary conditions, with and without damping.
AI.motivation: To study the chiral boundary value problem for the Landau-Lifshitz equation with helical derivatives, which is relevant for magnetic systems with helical structures and chiral boundary conditions.
AI.method: Introduce Sobolev spaces adapted to helical derivatives, establish energy estimates compatible with chiral boundary conditions, and prove global existence of weak solutions.
AI.result: Proved global existence of weak solutions to the Landau-Lifshitz equation with helical derivatives and chiral boundary conditions, both with and without damping terms.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes the mathematical foundation for studying Landau-Lifshitz equations with helical derivatives under chiral boundary conditions, providing existence results that work for both damped and undamped cases.

title: Long-time behavior of exact and numerical solutions of stochastic evolution equations on the sphere
summary: We investigate the long-time behavior of exact solutions and numerical approximations of linear stochastic evolution equations defined on the sphere. We focus on three classical models arising in mathematical physics: the stochastic wave equation, the stochastic Schrödinger equation, and the stochastic Maxwell's equations. For these SPDEs, we analyze several widely used time integrators with respect to trace formulas describing the evolution of physically relevant quantities such as energy, mass, and momentum dependent on the forcing term. In particular, we prove that the forward and backward Euler-Maruyama schemes fail to reproduce the correct long-time behavior of the exact solutions. In addition, we prove that the stochastic exponential integrator preserves the correct long-time behavior of the physical quantities of interest. Finally, several numerical experiments are provided to illustrate our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: Analysis of long-time behavior of exact solutions and numerical methods for linear stochastic evolution equations on the sphere, focusing on stochastic wave, Schrödinger, and Maxwell's equations.
AI.motivation: To understand how different numerical time integrators preserve or fail to preserve physically relevant quantities (energy, mass, momentum) in stochastic evolution equations on the sphere, which are important models in mathematical physics.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of exact solutions and numerical approximations for three stochastic PDEs on the sphere. Examination of forward/backward Euler-Maruyama schemes and stochastic exponential integrator with respect to trace formulas describing physical quantities.
AI.result: Forward and backward Euler-Maruyama schemes fail to reproduce correct long-time behavior of exact solutions. Stochastic exponential integrator preserves correct long-time behavior of physical quantities of interest.
AI.conclusion: For stochastic evolution equations on the sphere, careful choice of numerical integrator is crucial for preserving long-time behavior of physically relevant quantities; exponential integrators are superior to Euler-type methods for these problems.

title: Does the total energy difference method for modelling core level photoemission fail for bigger molecules?
summary: The $Δ$-Self-Consistent-Field ($Δ$SCF) method permits calculations of core electron binding energies in materials and molecules at a modest computational cost. However, it has been reported that whilst this method works well for small molecules, its accuracy drops off dramatically when larger systems are considered. Particularly large errors have been reported for the anthrone molecule, which consists of 25 atoms. In this work, the gas-phase photoelectron spectrum of anthrone is revisited both computationally and experimentally. The measured C 1s binding energies in anthrone differ markedly from previously published values, and the new experimental results are in good agreement with $Δ$SCF calculations based on the SCAN functional. In addition, the performance of the $Δ$SCF method is evaluated for a dataset of 44 core electron binding energies from medium sized molecules containing between 10 and 40 atoms. The mean absolute error for this dataset - 0.19 eV - is comparable to the results of previous computational benchmarks. Overall, these results and general theoretical considerations indicate that the $Δ$SCF method is suitable for modelling localized excitations in both small and large molecules, and applications to other extended systems are also promising.

AI.tldr: ΔSCF method for core electron binding energies works well for both small and large molecules, contrary to previous reports, with new experimental validation on anthrone showing good agreement.
AI.motivation: Previous reports suggested ΔSCF method accuracy drops dramatically for larger systems, particularly showing large errors for anthrone (25 atoms). This work aims to re-evaluate ΔSCF performance for medium-sized molecules.
AI.method: Revisited gas-phase photoelectron spectrum of anthrone computationally and experimentally. Evaluated ΔSCF performance on dataset of 44 core electron binding energies from molecules containing 10-40 atoms using SCAN functional.
AI.result: New experimental C 1s binding energies for anthrone differ from previously published values and show good agreement with ΔSCF/SCAN calculations. Mean absolute error for 44 core electron binding energies is 0.19 eV, comparable to previous benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ΔSCF method is suitable for modeling localized excitations in both small and large molecules, and applications to other extended systems are promising. Previous concerns about poor performance for larger systems were likely due to inaccurate experimental reference data.

title: Effects of Tungsten Radiative Cooling on Impurity, Heat and Momentum Transport in DIII-D Plasmas
summary: A first-of-its-kind experiment was conducted in the DIII-D tokamak under WEST similarity constraints on plasma shape and core parameters. This work presents a detailed transport study comparing a reference regime dominated by intrinsic carbon radiation and a high-radiation regime resulting from controlled tungsten (W) injection using the Laser Blow-Off system, with a core tungsten concentration $n_{\mathrm{W}}/n_e \sim 3\times 10^{-4}$ and a radiated-power fraction $f_\mathrm{rad}>0.5$. The W-induced radiative cooling lowered the electron temperature, thereby decreasing $T_e/T_i$ and stabilizing trapped-electron-mode (TEM) turbulence. This transition in turbulence regime reduced momentum and ion thermal diffusivities, yielding ion temperature peaking and a factor-of-two increase in toroidal rotation. At the outer plasma region, enhanced $E\timesB$ shear and increased collisionality further suppressed ion-scale turbulence, causing a sharp drop in ion heat flux. Consequently, impurity transport, predominantly turbulent in the low-radiation regime, acquired a strong neoclassical inward W convection during radiative cooling, bootstrapping the cooling cycle. Despite $f_\mathrm{rad}>0.5$, radiative collapse was not observed, likely owing to collisional ion-to-electron energy exchange acting as an electron-energy reservoir, together with $1/1$ MHD activity modulating the radiated power through core impurity neoclassical $T_i$-screening. These results support preparation for a tungsten wall change in DIII-D by elucidating tungsten-induced turbulence stabilization. They also provide key insights for interpreting plasma performance in WEST and are relevant to future reactors expected to operate with radiating tungsten-walled plasmas.

AI.tldr: Tungsten injection in DIII-D tokamak stabilizes turbulence, reduces heat transport, and increases rotation without radiative collapse, providing insights for tungsten-walled reactors.
AI.motivation: To understand tungsten-induced effects in tokamaks, particularly how tungsten injection affects turbulence, transport, and plasma performance, in preparation for DIII-D's tungsten wall change and to interpret WEST results.
AI.method: First-of-its-kind experiment in DIII-D tokamak comparing reference carbon-radiation regime with high-radiation regime from controlled tungsten injection using Laser Blow-Off system, achieving core tungsten concentration ~3×10⁻⁴ and radiated-power fraction >0.5.
AI.result: Tungsten radiative cooling lowered electron temperature, stabilized TEM turbulence, reduced momentum/ion thermal diffusivities, increased ion temperature peaking and toroidal rotation by factor of two. Enhanced E×B shear and collisionality suppressed ion-scale turbulence, causing sharp drop in ion heat flux. Impurity transport shifted from turbulent to strong neoclassical inward convection, bootstrapping cooling cycle. No radiative collapse observed despite high radiation.
AI.conclusion: Tungsten injection stabilizes turbulence through radiative cooling, improving confinement without radiative collapse due to collisional energy exchange and MHD activity. Results support DIII-D tungsten wall preparation and provide insights for WEST and future tungsten-walled reactors.

title: Polynomial Stability of an Elastic Thin Plate on Non-Smooth Domain
summary: This paper studies the polynomial stabilization of an elastic plate with dynamical boundary conditions on a non-smooth domain. To deal with the possible loss of solution regularity induced by boundary singularities, we formulate the problem as a precise variational framework. We prove that for domains with sufficiently small corner angles, the system retains the polynomial decay rate under standard geometric control conditions. In cases where larger corner angles lead to a significant regularity loss, we show that polynomial stability is recovered by introducing a feedback control at the corners.

AI.tldr: Polynomial stabilization of elastic plate with dynamical boundary conditions on non-smooth domains, addressing corner singularity effects.
AI.motivation: Study stabilization of elastic plates on non-smooth domains where boundary singularities (corners) can cause solution regularity loss, affecting stability properties.
AI.method: Formulate variational framework to handle boundary singularities, analyze domains with different corner angles, and introduce feedback control at corners for larger angles.
AI.result: For small corner angles, system retains polynomial decay under standard geometric control; for larger angles causing significant regularity loss, polynomial stability recovered via corner feedback control.
AI.conclusion: Corner angles significantly affect stability of elastic plates on non-smooth domains, but polynomial stabilization achievable through appropriate control strategies tailored to corner geometry.

title: Discrete Mean Field Games on Finite Graphs as Initial Value Optimization
summary: In this paper, we propose an initial value fomulation of the discrete mean field games on finite graphs (Graph MFG), and design a neural network based approach to solve it. Graph MFG describes infinite, non-cooperative and interactive homogeneous agents move on node states through the edges to optimize their own goals. Nash Equilibrium of the Graph MFG is characterized by a coupled ordinary differential equations (ODE) system, including the discrete forward continuity equation and the discrete backward Hamilton-Jacobi equation. In this paper, we mainly focus on the potential mean field games (Potential MFG) on finite graphs, which has an infinite-dimensional constrained optimization structure. We reformulate Potential MFG as an initial value finite-dimentional optimization problem with dynamics constrains, names Graph MFG-IV. Specifically, the initial condition of the Hamilton-Jacobi equation is regarded as the unique variable, constrained by the coupled Hamilton-Jacobi and continuity equation system as the ODE integrator. This formulation is a reduced-order model, which avoids time-discretization of the infinite-dimensional path and has a much smaller searching space than the general path-wise problem setting. We design a neural network-based approach to solve the Graph MFG-IV problem.

AI.tldr: Proposes Graph MFG-IV: an initial value formulation for potential mean field games on finite graphs, solved via neural networks.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient computational approach for solving discrete mean field games on graphs by reformulating them as initial value optimization problems, avoiding time-discretization of infinite-dimensional paths.
AI.method: Reformulates potential MFG on finite graphs as Graph MFG-IV: an initial value finite-dimensional optimization problem where the Hamilton-Jacobi equation's initial condition is the variable, constrained by coupled ODEs. Uses neural networks to solve this reduced-order model.
AI.result: Creates a reduced-order model with smaller search space than path-wise formulations, avoiding time-discretization of infinite-dimensional paths while maintaining the coupled ODE system structure.
AI.conclusion: The Graph MFG-IV formulation provides an efficient neural network-based approach for solving potential mean field games on finite graphs through initial value optimization rather than path discretization.

title: JZ-Tree: GPU friendly neighbour search and friends-of-friends with dual tree walks in JAX plus CUDA
comment: 13 pages, 9 figures, code available under https://github.com/jstuecker/jztree/
summary: Algorithms based on spatial tree traversal are widely regarded as among the most efficient and flexible approaches for many problems in CPU-based high-performance computing (HPC). However, directly transferring these algorithms to GPU architectures often yields substantially smaller performance gains than expected in light of the high computational throughput of modern GPUs.
