The branching nature of tree algorithms leads to thread divergence and irregular memory access patterns -- both of which may severely limit GPU performance. To address these challenges, we propose a Morton (z-order) 'plane-based tree hierarchy' that is specifically designed for GPU architectures. The resulting flattened data layout enables efficient dual-tree traversal with collaborative execution across thread groups, leading to highly coalesced memory access patterns. Based on this framework we present implementations of two important spatial algorithms -- exact $k$-nearest neighbour search and friends-of-friends (FoF) clustering. For both cases, we observe more than an order-of-magnitude performance improvement over the closest competing GPU libraries for large problem sizes ($N \gtrsim 10^7$), together with strong scaling to distributed multi-GPU systems. We provide an open-source implementation, 'JZ-Tree' (JAX z-order tree), which serves as a foundation for efficient GPU implementations of a broad class of tree-based algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Morton (z-order) 'plane-based tree hierarchy' for GPU architectures that enables efficient dual-tree traversal with coalesced memory access, achieving order-of-magnitude performance improvements for spatial algorithms like k-nearest neighbor search and friends-of-friends clustering.
AI.motivation: Spatial tree algorithms are efficient on CPUs but don't translate well to GPUs due to thread divergence and irregular memory access patterns caused by their branching nature, limiting GPU performance gains despite high computational throughput.
AI.method: Proposes a Morton (z-order) 'plane-based tree hierarchy' specifically designed for GPU architectures, creating a flattened data layout that enables efficient dual-tree traversal with collaborative execution across thread groups, leading to highly coalesced memory access patterns.
AI.result: More than order-of-magnitude performance improvement over competing GPU libraries for large problem sizes (N ≳ 10^7) for both k-nearest neighbor search and friends-of-friends clustering, with strong scaling to distributed multi-GPU systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GPU-optimized tree hierarchy enables efficient spatial algorithms on GPUs, addressing thread divergence and memory access issues. The open-source 'JZ-Tree' (JAX z-order tree) implementation serves as a foundation for efficient GPU implementations of a broad class of tree-based algorithms.

title: Kinetic magnetohydrodynamics and Landau fluid closure in relativity
comment: 23 pages + appendices. Supplementary notebook in the source file. Comments are welcome!
summary: Diffuse accretion flows near a supermassive black hole are fundamentally weakly collisional. In such weakly collisional plasmas, the ion and electron distribution functions can deviate significantly from thermal equilibrium, and particle kinetic effects can influence large-scale fluid motion by driving pressure anisotropy, heat conduction, and plasma instabilities. Modeling these plasma effects in highly relativistic flows could be important for interpreting horizon-scale observations of black hole images. In this paper, we present a theoretical framework for understanding weakly collisional plasmas in general relativity by deriving the relativistic drift kinetic equations from the Vlasov-Maxwell equations. We present the evolution equations for the moments of the gyroaveraged distribution function and introduce a new analytic Landau fluid closure to capture anisotropic heat flow in relativistic plasmas. Unlike standard (collisional) general relativistic magnetohydrodynamics or extended magnetohydrodynamics, our model does not rely on strong collisions to enforce thermal equilibrium and consistently incorporates Landau damping in a fluid closure. The model introduced in this work provides a complementary approach to fully kinetic simulations in understanding weakly collisional effects in low-luminosity relativistic black hole accretion disks.

AI.tldr: Derivation of relativistic drift kinetic equations for weakly collisional plasmas near black holes, with new Landau fluid closure to capture anisotropic heat flow without requiring thermal equilibrium.
AI.motivation: Diffuse accretion flows near supermassive black holes are weakly collisional, allowing distribution functions to deviate from thermal equilibrium. Particle kinetic effects can influence large-scale fluid motion through pressure anisotropy, heat conduction, and plasma instabilities. Modeling these effects is important for interpreting horizon-scale observations of black hole images.
AI.method: Derived relativistic drift kinetic equations from Vlasov-Maxwell equations. Developed evolution equations for moments of gyroaveraged distribution function. Introduced new analytic Landau fluid closure to capture anisotropic heat flow in relativistic plasmas.
AI.result: Created theoretical framework for understanding weakly collisional plasmas in general relativity. Model does not rely on strong collisions to enforce thermal equilibrium and consistently incorporates Landau damping in fluid closure.
AI.conclusion: Provides complementary approach to fully kinetic simulations for understanding weakly collisional effects in low-luminosity relativistic black hole accretion disks, addressing limitations of standard general relativistic magnetohydrodynamics.

title: The Stability of a Coupled Degenerate Wave System Under Boundary Control
summary: In this paper, we investigate a system composed of two degenerate wave equations which are connected at one point. By introducing some inequalities on the weighted spaces and employing the frequency domain method, we prove that the system is polynomially stable,which depends on the degree of the degeneracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proves polynomial stability for a system of two degenerate wave equations connected at a single point, with stability rate depending on degeneracy degree.
AI.motivation: To analyze stability properties of coupled degenerate wave equations, which are important in modeling physical systems with degeneracy and point connections.
AI.method: Uses weighted space inequalities and frequency domain method to analyze the system's stability properties.
AI.result: Proves the system is polynomially stable, with stability rate explicitly depending on the degree of degeneracy of the wave equations.
AI.conclusion: The coupled degenerate wave equation system exhibits polynomial stability rather than exponential stability, with degeneracy degree directly influencing the stability rate.

title: Robust H(curl)-based finite element methods for the incompressible MHD system
summary: We propose and analyze a class of finite element methods for the time-dependent incompressible magnetohydrodynamics system based on $H(\mathrm{curl})$-conforming discretizations for both the velocity and the magnetic field. This choice is guided by the aim of developing methods that are also suitable for the types of solutions arising in problems posed on nonconvex domains. Within this framework, we introduce three stabilized formulations, and study how the stabilization mechanisms employed influence their structural properties. In particular, we focus on suitability for nonconvex polyhedral domains, the need for Lagrange multipliers for the magnetic field, pressure-robustness, and quasi-robustness with respect to both the fluid and magnetic Reynolds numbers. The proposed formulations are further assessed through numerical experiments, highlighting their practical performance.

AI.tldr: H(curl)-conforming finite element methods for incompressible magnetohydrodynamics with stabilization for nonconvex domains.
AI.motivation: Develop methods suitable for solutions on nonconvex domains, addressing challenges in magnetohydrodynamics simulations for complex geometries.
AI.method: H(curl)-conforming discretizations for both velocity and magnetic field with three stabilized formulations, analyzing stabilization mechanisms and structural properties.
AI.result: Proposed formulations assessed through numerical experiments, demonstrating practical performance for nonconvex polyhedral domains.
AI.conclusion: H(curl)-conforming methods with appropriate stabilization provide robust solutions for magnetohydrodynamics on complex domains, addressing pressure-robustness and Reynolds number challenges.

title: Numerical study of probabilistic well-posedness of one dimensional fractional nonlinear wave equations
summary: The three dimensional cubic defocusing nonlinear wave equation is known to be ill-posed for general low regularity initial data. However, well-posedness can be recovered globally in time on a probabilistic level when considering random Gaussian initial data approximated by truncation of Fourier modes. These fine behaviors of nonlinear wave equations have not yet been observed numerically . In this article we perform numerical simulations of the one dimensional fractional cubic defocusing wave equation in a periodic setting. This allows us to explore energy subcritical and supercritial regimes. Our numerical results suggest that both norm inflation and probabilistic well-posedness can be observed numerically in energy sub-critical and super-critical regimes.

AI.tldr: Numerical simulations of 1D fractional cubic defocusing wave equation reveal both norm inflation and probabilistic well-posedness in energy subcritical and supercritical regimes.
AI.motivation: While 3D cubic defocusing nonlinear wave equation is known to be ill-posed for low regularity data but probabilistically well-posed for random Gaussian data, these behaviors haven't been numerically observed. The paper aims to numerically explore these phenomena in fractional wave equations.
AI.method: Numerical simulations of the one-dimensional fractional cubic defocusing wave equation in a periodic setting, allowing exploration of both energy subcritical and supercritical regimes.
AI.result: Numerical results show that both norm inflation (ill-posedness) and probabilistic well-posedness can be observed numerically in both energy sub-critical and super-critical regimes.
AI.conclusion: The study provides numerical evidence for theoretical predictions about probabilistic well-posedness and ill-posedness in fractional wave equations, bridging theoretical analysis with computational verification.

title: Electron Acceleration in a Flying-Focus Laser Wakefield Accelerator
summary: Structured light pulses hold significant promise for their ability to overcome dephasing in laser-wakefield accelerators, that should facilitate applications in high-energy physics and XFEL. Numerical studies have shown that sculpting a pulse into a flying focus and using it to drive a wakefield can achieve dephasing-free acceleration of electrons, with gain in excess of 100\,GeV within reachable with existing laser facilities. This work reports on novel experiments using a flying-focus generated laser-wakefield accelerator to accelerate electrons to relativistic energies. The flying-focus pulse is achieved by sculpting the laser-pulse before focusing using spatio-temporal couplings and generating a quasi-Bessel beam with an axiparabola. This combination allows for the tuning of the propagation velocity of the wakefield, which, we demonstrate, has an impact on the maximum achievable electron energy. Optical and particle-in-cell simulations are used to support the data and to provide direct evidence of the partial mitigation of dephasing through this flying-focus scheme. These results are further elucidated in our companion letter [1].

AI.tldr: Flying-focus laser pulses mitigate dephasing in laser-wakefield accelerators, enabling higher electron energies through tunable wakefield propagation velocity.
AI.motivation: Structured light pulses can overcome dephasing limitations in laser-wakefield accelerators, which is crucial for advancing high-energy physics and XFEL applications.
AI.method: Sculpting laser pulses into flying-focus using spatio-temporal couplings and axiparabola to generate quasi-Bessel beams, allowing tunable wakefield propagation velocity.
AI.result: Experimental demonstration of electron acceleration to relativistic energies with partial mitigation of dephasing, supported by optical and particle-in-cell simulations.
AI.conclusion: Flying-focus scheme enables tunable wakefield velocity that impacts maximum electron energy, providing a pathway toward dephasing-free acceleration exceeding 100 GeV.

title: Polynomial Stability of a Type II Porous Thermoelastic System with Local Memory Damping
summary: This paper studies the asymptotic behavior of a one-dimensional Type II porous thermoelastic system with a conservative porous structure and local memory damping applied to the elastic component. Using frequency domain resolvent estimates, we prove polynomial decay of the associated semigroup. Our results clarify the effect of local memory damping and provide a unified framework for partially damped porous thermoelastic systems

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes polynomial decay rates for a one-dimensional porous thermoelastic system with local memory damping on the elastic component, using frequency domain methods.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior and decay properties of porous thermoelastic systems with memory damping, particularly how local memory damping affects the system's stability and decay rates.
AI.method: Frequency domain resolvent estimates and semigroup theory to analyze the system's asymptotic behavior and prove polynomial decay rates.
AI.result: Proves polynomial decay of the associated semigroup, clarifies the effect of local memory damping, and provides a unified framework for partially damped porous thermoelastic systems.
AI.conclusion: Local memory damping applied to the elastic component of porous thermoelastic systems leads to polynomial decay, and the frequency domain approach offers a comprehensive framework for analyzing such partially damped systems.

title: On convergence of residual-based extended randomized Kaczmarz methods for matrix equations
summary: In this paper, for solving inconsistent matrix equations we propose a dual-space residual-based randomized extended Kaczmarz method and its version with Nesterov momentum. Without the full column rank assumptions on coefficient matrices, we provide a thorough convergence analysis, and derive upper bounds for the convergence rates of the new methods. A feasible range for the momentum parameters is determined. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed methods are much more effective than the existing ones, especially the method with momentum.

AI.tldr: New randomized Kaczmarz methods for solving inconsistent matrix equations without full column rank assumptions, with momentum acceleration and convergence analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for solving inconsistent matrix equations often require full column rank assumptions, limiting their applicability. There's a need for more effective methods that can handle rank-deficient coefficient matrices.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-space residual-based randomized extended Kaczmarz method and its version with Nesterov momentum acceleration. The methods work without requiring full column rank assumptions on coefficient matrices.
AI.result: Provides thorough convergence analysis with derived upper bounds for convergence rates. Determines a feasible range for momentum parameters. Numerical experiments show the proposed methods are much more effective than existing ones, especially the momentum version.
AI.conclusion: The new dual-space residual-based randomized extended Kaczmarz methods, particularly with Nesterov momentum, offer superior performance for solving inconsistent matrix equations without restrictive rank assumptions.

title: Composition design of refractory compositionally complex alloys using machine learning models
comment: 32 pages including 12 pages of SI, 6 figures in manuscript and 6 figures in SI, 50 references
summary: Refractory compositionally complex alloys (RCCAs) are considered the next generation high-temperature materials. However, their high-dimensional composition spaces are too large to explore by traditional density functional theory or experimental means, making new RCCA discovery slow and cumbersome. This work has addressed these challenges with an integrated composition design framework that can efficiently and exhaustively explore the relationship between the compositions and two fundamental aspects: 1) the phase stability, including the target body-centered cubic (BCC) phase and its competing phases (hexagonal closed-pack (HCP) structures, Laves and B2 intermetallic phases), and 2) the mechanical properties. This framework is demonstrated with RCCAs within nine refractory metals (Ti, V, Cr, Zr, Nb, Mo, Hf, Ta, and W). Theory-guided machine learning (ML) models were employed to find the composition-mechanical property relationship of RCCAs, where the established theory is used to supplement the yield strength data at ultra-high temperature, and a forward sequential feature selection (SFS) is used to determine feature selection. The resulting ML model for temperature-dependent yield strength was found to have an R_squared value of 0.98 over the entire temperature range (from 0 to 2000 K). The impact of each constituent element on the six key properties is evaluated. The addition of Nb tends to stabilize the BCC phase and the addition of Ti improves the ductility of RCCAs. Combined with all methods involved in this framework, the on-demand designer allows the alloy designers to have all properties for any RCCA compositions and narrow down the composition space by applying custom screening criteria. The output from the predictor and screener provides valuable guidance for our experimental study of RCCAs and accelerates the pace of materials discovery.

AI.tldr: Integrated ML framework for designing refractory compositionally complex alloys (RCCAs) by predicting phase stability and mechanical properties across composition space.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods (DFT/experiments) are too slow for exploring high-dimensional composition spaces of RCCAs, which are promising next-gen high-temperature materials.
AI.method: Theory-guided ML models with forward sequential feature selection to predict temperature-dependent yield strength (0-2000K) and phase stability for RCCAs with 9 refractory metals.
AI.result: ML model achieves R²=0.98 for yield strength prediction; identified Nb stabilizes BCC phase, Ti improves ductility; framework enables on-demand alloy design with custom screening.
AI.conclusion: Integrated framework accelerates RCCA discovery by efficiently exploring composition-property relationships and providing experimental guidance, overcoming traditional exploration limitations.

title: Monte-Carlo Event Generation for X-Ray Thomson Scattering Analysis
summary: A key diagnostic in warm-dense matter (WDM) experiments is X-ray Thomson scattering (XRTS), but its interpretation is often limited by complex instrument effects and the high computationally expensive combinations of microscopic models with detector simulations. We present a proof-of-principle implementation of an event-driven approach to XRTS modelling, inspired by particle physics event-generators. Instead of computing the spectra via forward models, individual scattering events are sampled from the differential cross section and sent through a spectrometer simulation. This provides a statistically consistent representation that preserves full kinematic information and enables flexible and geometry-aware analysis. We demonstrate the feasibility and physical consistency of the method for non-resonant XRTS in a synthetic setup. By decoupling event generation from detector-level analysis, the framework allows efficient reuse of the sampled events and reduces computational overhead associated with repeated evaluations. The method is model-agnostic and establishes a new connection between particle-physics event generation techniques and WDM diagnostics, providing a scalable foundation for advanced XRTS analysis and inference.

AI.tldr: Event-driven approach to X-ray Thomson scattering modeling using particle physics event-generator techniques for more efficient and flexible WDM diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Current XRTS interpretation is limited by complex instrument effects and high computational costs from combining microscopic models with detector simulations.
AI.method: Event-driven approach inspired by particle physics event-generators: sample individual scattering events from differential cross section and send through spectrometer simulation instead of computing spectra via forward models.
AI.result: Proof-of-principle implementation demonstrates feasibility and physical consistency for non-resonant XRTS in synthetic setup. Method preserves full kinematic information and enables flexible, geometry-aware analysis.
AI.conclusion: Event-driven framework decouples event generation from detector-level analysis, allowing efficient reuse of sampled events, reducing computational overhead, and establishing new connection between particle physics techniques and WDM diagnostics.

title: Positive Solutions for a Mixed Local-Nonlocal Problem with Semipositone Nonlinearity

AI.tldr: Existence of positive solution for mixed local-nonlocal semipositone problem using mountain pass arguments and comparison principles.
AI.motivation: Study mixed local-nonlocal semipositone problems combining p-Laplacian and fractional p-Laplacian operators, which arise in various applications including phase transitions and population dynamics.
AI.method: Use mountain pass arguments, comparison principles, and regularity principles to prove existence of positive solutions.
AI.result: Prove existence of at least one positive solution for the mixed local-nonlocal semipositone problem with appropriate boundary conditions.
AI.conclusion: Successfully establish existence theory for mixed local-nonlocal semipositone problems using variational methods and comparison techniques.

title: Linear convergence of Gearhart-Koshy accelerated Kaczmarz methods for tensor linear systems
summary: The generalized Gearhart-Koshy acceleration is a recent exact affine search technique designed for the method of cyclic projections onto hyperplanes, i.e., the Kaczmarz method. However, its convergence properties, particularly the linear convergence rate, have not been thoroughly established. In this paper, we systematically establish the linear convergence of the generalized Gearhart-Koshy accelerated Kaczmarz method for tensor linear systems, proving that it converges linearly to the unique least-norm solution. Our analysis is general and applies to several popular Kaczmarz variants, including incremental, shuffle-once, and random-reshuffling schemes, and demonstrates that this acceleration approach yields a better convergence upper bound compared to the plain Kaczmarz method. We also propose an efficient Gram-Schmidt-based implementation that computes the next iterate in linear time. Building on this implementation, we establish a connection between this acceleration framework and Arnoldi-type Krylov subspace methods, further highlighting its efficiency and potential. Our theoretical results are supported by numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes linear convergence for the generalized Gearhart-Koshy accelerated Kaczmarz method for tensor linear systems, showing it converges to the unique least-norm solution with better convergence bounds than plain Kaczmarz.
AI.motivation: The generalized Gearhart-Koshy acceleration is a recent exact affine search technique for the Kaczmarz method, but its convergence properties, particularly linear convergence rate, have not been thoroughly established.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of linear convergence for generalized Gearhart-Koshy accelerated Kaczmarz method for tensor linear systems, with analysis covering incremental, shuffle-once, and random-reshuffling variants. Also proposes efficient Gram-Schmidt-based implementation and connects the acceleration framework to Arnoldi-type Krylov subspace methods.
AI.result: Proves linear convergence to unique least-norm solution, shows acceleration yields better convergence upper bound than plain Kaczmarz, demonstrates efficient linear-time implementation, and establishes connection to Krylov subspace methods.
AI.conclusion: The generalized Gearhart-Koshy acceleration provides theoretically sound and practically efficient acceleration for Kaczmarz-type methods with linear convergence guarantees, supported by numerical experiments.

title: Numerically Exact Study of Flat-Band Superconductivity
summary: Current theories of high-temperature superconductivity in flat-band systems predict a linear dependence of the transition temperature on the attractive interaction, $T_c(U) = c|U|$. However, neither the value of $c$ nor the full nonlinear $T_c(U)$ curve -- with a maximum at large $|U|$ -- is known beyond mean-field and quantum geometry estimates. Using a controlled diagrammatic Monte Carlo technique, we trace the onset of superfluid response in the Lieb lattice with attractive Hubbard interaction. Focusing on the half-filled flat-band case, where the ordering mechanism differs fundamentally from both BCS and preformed Cooper pair scenarios, we find that the pairing response diverges linearly with decreasing temperature over a broad range of $U$, leading to a sharp crossover to long-range correlations at a characteristic temperature $T_*$, which provides a controlled upper bound on $T_c$. The highest $T_*$ occurs when all three bands touch at a single momentum point, potentially corresponding to high $T_c$ values.

AI.tldr: Monte Carlo study of superconductivity in Lieb lattice flat band reveals linear divergence of pairing response and sharp crossover temperature T_* that bounds T_c, with highest T_* when bands touch at single momentum point.
AI.motivation: Current theories predict linear T_c dependence on interaction strength (T_c(U)=c|U|) but lack knowledge of coefficient c and full nonlinear T_c(U) curve beyond mean-field approximations, especially for flat-band systems where ordering differs from BCS and preformed pair scenarios.
AI.method: Used controlled diagrammatic Monte Carlo technique to trace onset of superfluid response in Lieb lattice with attractive Hubbard interaction, focusing on half-filled flat-band case.
AI.result: Found pairing response diverges linearly with decreasing temperature over broad U range, leading to sharp crossover to long-range correlations at characteristic temperature T_* that provides controlled upper bound on T_c. Highest T_* occurs when all three bands touch at single momentum point.
AI.conclusion: The study provides controlled numerical evidence for superconductivity in flat-band systems, revealing T_* as upper bound for T_c and identifying optimal conditions (band touching at single momentum point) for potentially high T_c values.

title: Fully nonlinear elliptic PDEs in thin domains with oblique-Dirichlet mixed boundary conditions
summary: We consider asymptotic behavior of solutions to the oblique-Dirichlet mixed boundary conditions without the strict monotonicity of the equation in the variable corresponding to the unknown function for "thin domains" i.e. when the N+1 dimensional domains collapse to an N dimensional domain. A global ellipticity condition in the limit equation is introduced.

AI.tldr: Analysis of PDE solutions in thin domains collapsing to lower dimensions, focusing on oblique-Dirichlet mixed boundary conditions without strict monotonicity, using global ellipticity condition.
AI.motivation: To understand asymptotic behavior of PDE solutions when domains collapse from N+1 to N dimensions, particularly for oblique-Dirichlet mixed boundary conditions without requiring strict monotonicity assumptions.
AI.method: Introduces a global ellipticity condition for the limit equation to handle the collapse of thin domains, analyzing solutions under oblique-Dirichlet mixed boundary conditions without strict monotonicity.
AI.result: Establishes asymptotic behavior results for solutions in collapsing thin domains using the global ellipticity condition framework.
AI.conclusion: The global ellipticity condition enables analysis of PDE solutions in thin domain limits without requiring strict monotonicity, providing a framework for oblique-Dirichlet mixed boundary problems.

title: Overview of Bayesian Solvers in EEG Distributed Source Models: Prior Selection, Algorithmic Implementation, and Depth Bias Reduction
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) source imaging aims to reconstruct the spatial distribution of neural activity within the brain from non-invasive scalp measurements. This inverse problem is severely ill-posed due to the low spatial resolution of EEG and the presence of measurement noise, necessitating robust regularization techniques. Bayesian approaches provide a principled framework for incorporating prior knowledge into the solution, where regularization naturally arises through prior distributions and their associated hyperparameters. In this work, we provide an overview of key Bayesian methods for EEG source imaging based on Gaussian, Laplace, and group Laplace priors, with particular emphasis on hierarchical models that promote sparsity. We analyze the connections between these hierarchical formulations and classical optimization techniques, and provide an analytical description of their implementation using expectation -maximization and alternating optimization algorithms. To address the issue of depth bias where deeper sources are systematically underestimated or mislocalized - we extend a statistical signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) framework to derive depth-weighted priors that account for differences in how strongly sources at different depths are reflected in the measurements. Finally, we illustrate the behaviour of the considered models through simulation studies involving sources at varying depths. The results highlight the impact of prior selection and depth weighting on reconstruction accuracy and demonstrate the importance of informed model design for depth-sensitive EEG source localization.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of Bayesian methods for EEG source imaging, focusing on hierarchical models with sparsity-promoting priors (Gaussian, Laplace, group Laplace), their connections to optimization techniques, and introduces depth-weighted priors to address depth bias in source localization.
AI.motivation: EEG source imaging is an ill-posed inverse problem due to low spatial resolution and measurement noise. Bayesian approaches offer a principled framework for regularization, but depth bias (systematic underestimation of deeper sources) remains a critical challenge that needs to be addressed for accurate source localization.
AI.method: The paper reviews Bayesian methods using Gaussian, Laplace, and group Laplace priors with hierarchical models promoting sparsity. It analyzes connections to classical optimization techniques, implements models using expectation-maximization and alternating optimization algorithms, and extends a statistical SNR framework to derive depth-weighted priors that account for source depth variations.
AI.result: Simulation studies with sources at varying depths demonstrate that prior selection and depth weighting significantly impact reconstruction accuracy. The depth-weighted priors effectively address depth bias, improving localization of deeper sources that are typically underestimated in standard approaches.
AI.conclusion: Informed model design with appropriate prior selection and depth weighting is crucial for depth-sensitive EEG source localization. The hierarchical Bayesian framework with depth-weighted priors provides an effective solution to the depth bias problem, enhancing the accuracy and reliability of EEG source imaging.

title: Asymptotic models for viscoelastic one-dimensional blood flow
summary: We derive a unidirectional asymptotic model for one-dimensional blood flow in viscoelastic arteries. We prove local well-posedness of strong solutions in Sobolev spaces for general parameters and mean-zero periodic data. In the purely elastic BBM regime we further establish global existence and exponential decay for sufficiently small initial data. We also present a numerical study of the reduced model, including comparisons across different viscoelastic and amplitude regimes, and discuss the observed dynamics in connection with the continuation criterion.

AI.tldr: Unidirectional asymptotic model for 1D blood flow in viscoelastic arteries with local well-posedness, global existence for small data in elastic regime, and numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To develop a simplified unidirectional model for blood flow in viscoelastic arteries that captures essential dynamics while being mathematically tractable for analysis and numerical simulation.
AI.method: Derived a unidirectional asymptotic model for 1D blood flow in viscoelastic arteries, proved local well-posedness in Sobolev spaces for general parameters with periodic data, established global existence and exponential decay for small initial data in purely elastic BBM regime, and conducted numerical studies across different viscoelastic and amplitude regimes.
AI.result: Successfully derived the model, proved local well-posedness for general parameters, established global existence and exponential decay for small data in elastic regime, and validated the model numerically with comparisons across different regimes.
AI.conclusion: The unidirectional asymptotic model provides a mathematically rigorous and computationally efficient framework for studying blood flow in viscoelastic arteries, with proven well-posedness properties and numerical validation across different physical regimes.

title: On the submatrices with the best-bounded inverses
comment: Preprint; order of authors was determined by a coin flip
summary: The following hypothesis was formulated by Goreinov, Tyrtyshnikov, and Zamarashkin in \cite{goreinov1997theory}. If $U$ is $n\times k$ real matrix with the orthonormal columns $(n>k)$, then there exists a submatrix $Q$ of $U$ of size $k\times k$ such that its smallest singular value is at least $\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}.$ Although this statement is supported by numerical experiments, the problem remains open for all $1<k<n-1,$ except for the case of $n \leq 4,\ k=2.$ In this work, we provide a proof for the case $k=2.$

AI.tldr: The paper proves the Goreinov-Tyrtyshnikov-Zamarashkin hypothesis for the specific case k=2, which states that for any n×2 real matrix with orthonormal columns, there exists a 2×2 submatrix with smallest singular value at least 1/√n.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address an open problem in matrix theory: proving the existence of well-conditioned submatrices in orthonormal matrices. This has implications for numerical linear algebra, particularly in applications requiring column subset selection and matrix approximations.
AI.method: The authors provide a mathematical proof for the specific case k=2. While the abstract doesn't detail the proof technique, it likely involves geometric arguments about orthonormal matrices, singular value analysis, and combinatorial selection of submatrices.
AI.result: The main result is a proof that for any n×2 real matrix U with orthonormal columns (n>2), there exists a 2×2 submatrix Q such that its smallest singular value is at least 1/√n.
AI.conclusion: This work resolves the open problem for the k=2 case, providing theoretical justification for the existence of well-conditioned 2×2 submatrices in any orthonormal matrix, while the general case for 1<k<n-1 remains open.

title: The Navier-Stokes equations in $\mathbb R^2_+$ with point vortex initial data: construction of the solution
summary: This is the first of two papers concerning the asymptotic behavior of the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations in a half-space at high Reynolds numbers, with initial data given by a point vortex. In the present work, we establish the existence and uniqueness of solutions subject to the non-slip boundary condition. This result was established in \cite{Ken} under the condition that the total mass is sufficiently small. Here, we eliminate the smallness assumption by analyzing the linearized operator near the point vortex and constructing a tailored functional framework-one designed to capture the distinct behaviors of the solution in the vicinity of the point vortex and the boundary, respectively.

AI.tldr: Existence and uniqueness of Navier-Stokes solutions in half-space with point vortex initial data, removing previous smallness assumption through tailored functional analysis.
AI.motivation: To establish existence and uniqueness for incompressible Navier-Stokes equations in half-space with point vortex initial data and no-slip boundary conditions, eliminating the previous requirement for small total mass.
AI.method: Analyze linearized operator near point vortex and construct tailored functional framework to capture distinct behaviors near vortex and boundary separately.
AI.result: Proved existence and uniqueness of solutions without smallness assumption on total mass, overcoming limitation of previous work.
AI.conclusion: Successfully established rigorous mathematical foundation for Navier-Stokes with point vortex in half-space using specialized functional analysis approach.

title: A boundary integral equation method for Steklov eigenvalue problems for smooth planar domains
summary: In this paper, we study the computational question of whether the Steklov spectrum of smooth simply connected planar domains can be approximated accurately by a boundary-only formulation based on harmonic conjugation. For the unit disk, the Dirichlet-to-Neumann operator can be written explicitly in terms of the classical conjugation operator. We show how this viewpoint extends to general bounded and unbounded simply connected domains through the generalized conjugation operator defined through the boundary integral equation with the generalized Neumann kernel. Combined with Fourier differentiation on an equidistant boundary grid, this leads to a dense algebraic eigenvalue problem for the boundary traces of Steklov eigenfunctions. The resulting method uses only boundary data, treats interior and exterior problems in a unified way, and reconstructs eigenfunctions in the domain by harmonic extension. Numerical experiments on benchmark domains and on parameter-dependent smooth families, including ellipses and star-like curves, show high accuracy for smooth boundaries and illustrate how the Steklov spectrum changes with geometry.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a boundary-only numerical method for approximating Steklov spectra of planar domains using harmonic conjugation and Fourier differentiation, achieving high accuracy for smooth boundaries.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient computational method for approximating Steklov spectra that uses only boundary data, avoids interior discretization, and works for both bounded and unbounded simply connected domains in a unified way.
AI.method: Uses harmonic conjugation through the generalized Neumann kernel boundary integral equation to formulate the Dirichlet-to-Neumann operator. Combines this with Fourier differentiation on equidistant boundary grids to create a dense algebraic eigenvalue problem for boundary traces of Steklov eigenfunctions. Eigenfunctions are reconstructed by harmonic extension.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on benchmark domains and parameter-dependent smooth families (ellipses, star-like curves) show high accuracy for smooth boundaries and demonstrate how the Steklov spectrum changes with geometry.
AI.conclusion: The boundary-only formulation based on harmonic conjugation provides an effective method for computing Steklov spectra, offering geometric flexibility and high accuracy for smooth boundaries while avoiding interior discretization.

title: The Navier-Stokes equations in $\mathbb R^2_+$ with point vortex initial data: Zero-viscosity limit
summary: This is the second of two papers devoted to the asymptotic behavior of solutions to the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations in a half-space with point vortex initial data. A major difficulty stems from the interaction between the point vortex initial data and the boundary, which complicates the derivation of a valid asymptotic expansion. To overcome this, we carry out a precise matching between the point vortex and boundary-layer profiles to accurately capture the correct viscous behavior of the vortex in the half-plane. Based on this matched asymptotic analysis, we decompose the vorticity into three components: vorticity near the point vortex, vorticity near the boundary, and vorticity in the transition layer. A key point is that each component must be analyzed in its own distinct region. On this basis, we establish refined estimates and thereby achieve the inviscid limit for the point vortex. Finally, we rigorously prove that solutions to the Navier-Stokes equations converge to the Lamb-Oseen vortex away from the boundary, while approaching the Prandtl boundary-layer system in the near-boundary region.

AI.tldr: Asymptotic analysis of Navier-Stokes equations in half-space with point vortex initial data, showing convergence to Lamb-Oseen vortex away from boundary and Prandtl boundary-layer near boundary.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior of solutions to incompressible Navier-Stokes equations in half-space with point vortex initial data, particularly addressing the challenging interaction between point vortex and boundary effects.
AI.method: Precise matching between point vortex and boundary-layer profiles, decomposition of vorticity into three components (near vortex, near boundary, transition layer), refined estimates in distinct regions, and matched asymptotic analysis.
AI.result: Established inviscid limit for point vortex, proved convergence to Lamb-Oseen vortex away from boundary and to Prandtl boundary-layer system in near-boundary region.
AI.conclusion: Successfully resolved the complex interaction between point vortex and boundary through matched asymptotic analysis, providing rigorous asymptotic behavior of Navier-Stokes solutions in half-space with point vortex initial data.

title: A Neural-Enhanced Weak Galerkin Method for Second-Order Elliptic Problems with Low-Regularity Solutions
summary: We propose a neural-enhanced weak Galerkin (WG) finite element method for second-order elliptic problems with low-regularity solutions. The method augments the classical WG approximation space with neural network functions constructed via a residual-driven Galerkin enrichment procedure. This approach preserves the variational structure, symmetry, and stability of the WG formulation while enhancing its ability to approximate non-smooth and singular solution components. We establish a quasi-optimal error estimate in a discrete WG energy norm, incorporating both projection and consistency errors. In particular, the method retains optimal convergence rates for smooth solutions. For problems admitting a regular--singular decomposition, we further show that the neural enrichment effectively captures the singular component, yielding improved accuracy over standard WG methods.

AI.tldr: Neural-enhanced weak Galerkin finite element method combines classical WG with neural networks via residual-driven enrichment for elliptic problems with low-regularity solutions.
AI.motivation: To improve approximation of non-smooth and singular solution components in second-order elliptic problems while maintaining the desirable properties of weak Galerkin methods.
AI.method: Augments classical WG approximation space with neural network functions using residual-driven Galerkin enrichment procedure, preserving variational structure, symmetry, and stability of WG formulation.
AI.result: Establishes quasi-optimal error estimate in discrete WG energy norm, retains optimal convergence rates for smooth solutions, and shows neural enrichment effectively captures singular components for problems with regular-singular decomposition.
AI.conclusion: The neural-enhanced WG method provides improved accuracy over standard WG methods for low-regularity elliptic problems while maintaining theoretical guarantees and structural properties.

title: On the pointwise convergence of NLS flow on $ §^2 $

AI.tldr: This paper proves almost sure pointwise convergence almost everywhere for cubic NLS on S² at low regularity using randomization methods, and establishes a new necessary condition for L^p maximal estimates of linear Schrödinger equation on S².
AI.motivation: The motivation is to extend previous results on almost everywhere convergence for nonlinear Schrödinger equations from Euclidean spaces to the spherical setting (S²), and to improve understanding of L^p maximal estimates for linear Schrödinger equations on spheres.
AI.method: The authors use randomization methods and an ansatz introduced by Burq, Camps, Sun, and Tzvetkov. They apply these techniques to study the cubic nonlinear Schrödinger equation on S² and analyze L^p maximal estimates for the linear Schrödinger equation on S².
AI.result: 1) Proved almost sure pointwise convergence almost everywhere for cubic NLS on S² at very low regularity. 2) Showed that L^p maximal estimate fails for s < 1/2 - 1/(2p) with p ≥ 2. 3) For p=3, the result matches the corresponding range in ℝ² case (up to endpoint) and improves previous results by Chen-Duong-Lee-Yan.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully extends Compaan-Lucà-Staffilani's results to the spherical setting and provides improved necessary conditions for L^p maximal estimates on S², establishing connections between spherical and Euclidean cases for Schrödinger equations.

title: QAFE$^2$: Quantum Accelerated Multiscale Finite Element Analysis
summary: The computational cost of concurrent multiscale finite element methods is dominated by the repeated solution of microscopic representative volume element (RVE) problems at macroscopic quadrature points. In this work, we introduce a quantum-classical framework for multiscale finite element analysis (QAFE$^2$) that leverages quantum parallelism to fundamentally alter the scaling of RVE-based homogenisation. At the single-RVE level, the proposed quantum solver attains polylogarithmic complexity with respect to the microscopic discretisation size, yielding an exponential asymptotic speedup over the best available classical solvers. More importantly, QAFE$^2$ exploits quantum superposition and entanglement to evaluate, in a single quantum execution, the entire ensemble of RVE problems associated with all macroscopic quadrature points. This capability is a form of intrinsic quantum concurrency with no classical analogue. Numerical experiments on one- and two-dimensional model problems with known analytical solutions confirm the accuracy of the proposed formulation and verify the theoretical computational scaling and parallel performance.

AI.tldr: Quantum-classical framework (QAFE²) uses quantum computing to solve multiscale finite element RVE problems with exponential speedup and quantum parallelism for concurrent evaluation.
AI.motivation: The computational bottleneck in concurrent multiscale finite element methods is the repeated solution of microscopic RVE problems at macroscopic quadrature points, which dominates computational costs.
AI.method: Introduces QAFE² framework combining quantum and classical computing: quantum solver achieves polylogarithmic complexity for single RVE problems, and exploits quantum superposition/entanglement to evaluate all RVE problems across quadrature points in a single quantum execution.
AI.result: Quantum solver achieves exponential asymptotic speedup over classical solvers; quantum parallelism enables concurrent evaluation of all RVE problems; numerical experiments on 1D/2D model problems confirm accuracy and verify theoretical computational scaling and parallel performance.
AI.conclusion: QAFE² fundamentally alters the scaling of RVE-based homogenization through quantum parallelism, offering intrinsic quantum concurrency with no classical analogue for multiscale finite element analysis.

title: Refined boundary layer asymptotics for elliptic equations with multiplicative nonlocal effects
summary: We investigate singularly perturbed elliptic problems with multiplicative nonlocal diffusion terms subject to Robin boundary conditions. The diffusion depends on a global quantity of the solution, which introduces a nonlocal coupling between the global behavior of the solution and the boundary asymptotics. As the perturbation parameter tends to zero, we establish precise asymptotic expansions of the solutions that capture the structure of boundary layers coupled with the multiplicative nonlocal diffusion effect. Moreover, the interaction between the nonlocal diffusion and the boundary geometry manifests as refined higher-order terms wherein geometric quantities, such as the mean curvature, appear explicitly; our analysis thus quantifies the influence of global coupling on the boundary layer structure, extending classical singular perturbation theory to multiplicative nonlocal frameworks.

AI.tldr: Singularly perturbed elliptic problems with multiplicative nonlocal diffusion and Robin boundary conditions: asymptotic expansions reveal boundary layers coupled with nonlocal effects, with geometric quantities like mean curvature appearing in higher-order terms.
AI.motivation: Extend classical singular perturbation theory to multiplicative nonlocal frameworks where diffusion depends on global solution quantities, creating coupling between global behavior and boundary asymptotics.
AI.method: Investigate singularly perturbed elliptic problems with multiplicative nonlocal diffusion terms subject to Robin boundary conditions, establishing asymptotic expansions as perturbation parameter tends to zero.
AI.result: Precise asymptotic expansions capture boundary layer structure coupled with multiplicative nonlocal diffusion effect; geometric quantities like mean curvature appear explicitly in refined higher-order terms.
AI.conclusion: Analysis quantifies influence of global coupling on boundary layer structure, extending classical singular perturbation theory to multiplicative nonlocal frameworks with geometric interactions.

title: Exponential mixing for nonlinear Schrödinger equations perturbed by bounded degenerate noise
summary: We prove the exponential convergence to a unique invariant measure for locally damped nonlinear Schrödinger equations, perturbed by bounded noise acting on only two Fourier modes. To tackle the lack of smoothing effect, we introduce asymptotic compactness of linearized system to enhance the coupling method. Inspired by [14,33,39], we establish a new criterion for exponential mixing. Elements from global stability, nonlinear smoothing, and geometric control are combined when applying this criterion.

AI.tldr: Exponential convergence to unique invariant measure for locally damped nonlinear Schrödinger equations with bounded noise on two Fourier modes, using asymptotic compactness to enhance coupling method.
AI.motivation: To prove exponential mixing for nonlinear Schrödinger equations with local damping and limited noise (only two Fourier modes), addressing the challenge of lack of smoothing effect in the system.
AI.method: Introduce asymptotic compactness of linearized system to enhance the coupling method; establish new criterion for exponential mixing inspired by previous works; combine elements from global stability, nonlinear smoothing, and geometric control.
AI.result: Prove exponential convergence to a unique invariant measure for locally damped nonlinear Schrödinger equations perturbed by bounded noise acting on only two Fourier modes.
AI.conclusion: Successfully overcome the lack of smoothing effect through asymptotic compactness and new mixing criterion, demonstrating that limited noise (two Fourier modes) is sufficient for exponential mixing in locally damped nonlinear Schrödinger systems.

title: Curvature-Aware Optimization for High-Accuracy Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Efficient and robust optimization is essential for neural networks, enabling scientific machine learning models to converge rapidly to very high accuracy -- faithfully capturing complex physical behavior governed by differential equations. In this work, we present advanced optimization strategies to accelerate the convergence of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for challenging partial (PDEs) and ordinary differential equations (ODEs). Specifically, we provide efficient implementations of the Natural Gradient (NG) optimizer, Self-Scaling BFGS and Broyden optimizers, and demonstrate their performance on problems including the Helmholtz equation, Stokes flow, inviscid Burgers equation, Euler equations for high-speed flows, and stiff ODEs arising in pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. Beyond optimizer development, we also propose new PINN-based methods for solving the inviscid Burgers and Euler equations, and compare the resulting solutions against high-order numerical methods to provide a rigorous and fair assessment. Finally, we address the challenge of scaling these quasi-Newton optimizers for batched training, enabling efficient and scalable solutions for large data-driven problems.

AI.tldr: Advanced optimization strategies for PINNs including Natural Gradient, Self-Scaling BFGS, and Broyden optimizers to accelerate convergence for challenging PDEs and ODEs, with applications to Helmholtz, Stokes flow, Burgers, Euler equations, and stiff ODEs.
AI.motivation: Efficient and robust optimization is essential for neural networks to enable scientific machine learning models to converge rapidly to high accuracy for capturing complex physical behavior governed by differential equations.
AI.method: Developed efficient implementations of Natural Gradient optimizer, Self-Scaling BFGS and Broyden optimizers; proposed new PINN-based methods for solving inviscid Burgers and Euler equations; addressed scaling challenges for batched training.
AI.result: Demonstrated performance on challenging problems including Helmholtz equation, Stokes flow, inviscid Burgers equation, Euler equations for high-speed flows, and stiff ODEs in pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics; compared solutions against high-order numerical methods.
AI.conclusion: Advanced optimization strategies enable accelerated convergence of PINNs for challenging differential equations, with scalable quasi-Newton optimizers providing efficient solutions for large data-driven problems.

title: Quantitative stability of constant equilibria in a non-linear alignment model of self-propelled particles
summary: We are interested in the long-time behaviour of the kinetic Vicsek equation, rigorously derived as the mean-field limit~\cite{bolley2012meanfield} of a coupled system of~$N$ stochastic differential equations describing particles moving at unit velocity and aligning with their neighbours. We focus on the local-in-space version (that may for instance appear as a moderate interaction limit instead of mean-field), which is not a priori globally well-posed and could explode in finite time. Despite its simple expression, little is rigorously established about the behaviour of its solutions. We use hypocoercivity methods to show that finite time explosion does not occur in the vicinity of uniform and homogeneous equilibria in space below the critical threshold. We recast the now-classic~\cite{villani2009hypocoercivity} approach of modifying Sobolev-type norms by adding cross-terms, linked to commutators between the different operators appearing in the kinetic equation. However, the fact that the velocity space is the sphere adds significant subtleties and requires to develop an adapted algebraic framework of operators. Taking advantage of this new framework, we manage to perform an approach \textit{à la} Hérau~\cite{herau2007short} to show the nonlinear stability. Our main results are a quantitative decay estimate in the case of the whole space, despite the absence of control of the $L^1$ norm of the perturbation, and a gain in regularity at the nonlinear level which allows to have well-posedness and stability in the space~$H^{s,0}(\mathbb{R}^d\times§)$ for some~$s<\frac{d}2$ (that is to say without a priori uniform bound in space on the~$L^2$ norm in velocity, that would come with Sobolev injection in the case~$s>\frac{d}2$).

AI.tldr: The paper studies the long-time behavior of the kinetic Vicsek equation, showing that finite-time explosion does not occur near uniform equilibria below critical threshold using hypocoercivity methods and a new algebraic framework for the spherical velocity space.
AI.motivation: The kinetic Vicsek equation, derived as a mean-field limit of particle systems with alignment interactions, lacks rigorous understanding of solution behavior and could potentially explode in finite time. The authors aim to establish stability properties near uniform equilibria.
AI.method: The authors use hypocoercivity methods with modified Sobolev-type norms containing cross-terms, developing a new algebraic framework to handle the spherical velocity space. They employ an approach à la Hérau for nonlinear stability analysis.
AI.result: Main results include: 1) Quantitative decay estimates for the whole space case despite lack of L¹ norm control, 2) Gain in regularity at nonlinear level enabling well-posedness and stability in H^{s,0} spaces for s < d/2, 3) Proof that finite-time explosion does not occur near uniform equilibria below critical threshold.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes stability properties of the kinetic Vicsek equation near uniform equilibria using hypocoercivity techniques adapted to the spherical velocity space, providing important insights into the long-time behavior of this alignment model.

title: Formalization of De Giorgi--Nash--Moser Theory in Lean
summary: We present a formalization in Lean of the core interior De Giorgi--Nash--Moser theory for uniformly elliptic divergence-form equations with bounded measurable coefficients. The formalized results include local boundedness of weak subsolutions, the weak Harnack inequality for positive weak supersolutions, Moser's Harnack inequality for positive weak solutions, and interior Hölder regularity. This is, to our knowledge, the first machine-checked formalization of a major theorem in modern PDE theory. The development also required substantial new infrastructure for Sobolev spaces on bounded domains, weak solutions of elliptic equations, and quantitative regularity estimates. More broadly, it suggests that large-scale autoformalization of hard analysis in Lean is now within reach.

AI.tldr: First machine-checked formalization in Lean of core interior De Giorgi-Nash-Moser theory for elliptic PDEs with bounded measurable coefficients.
AI.motivation: To create the first machine-checked formalization of a major theorem in modern PDE theory, demonstrating that large-scale autoformalization of hard analysis in Lean is now feasible.
AI.method: Formalization in Lean theorem prover of the interior De Giorgi-Nash-Moser theory for uniformly elliptic divergence-form equations with bounded measurable coefficients, including development of substantial new infrastructure for Sobolev spaces, weak solutions, and quantitative regularity estimates.
AI.result: Successfully formalized: local boundedness of weak subsolutions, weak Harnack inequality for positive weak supersolutions, Moser's Harnack inequality for positive weak solutions, and interior Hölder regularity.
AI.conclusion: This represents a significant milestone as the first machine-checked formalization of major PDE theory, suggesting that large-scale autoformalization of hard analysis in Lean is now achievable.

title: Gauge Symmetry Breaking in the Asymptotic Analysis of Self Dual Yang-Mills-Higgs $SU(2)$ Monopoles
summary: We consider the $SU(2)$ Self-Dual Yang Mills Higgs Lagrangian in 3 dimension. By adding a ''Gauge Mass'' term to this YMH Lagrangian in the form of $L^2$ norm of the connection we break the gauge invariance and critical points are automatically fulfilling globally the Coulomb condition. We study the so called ``large mass asymptotic'', which has the effect of ''squeezing'' the monopoles. For any unit Higgs field data at the boundary we prove that minimizers of this Coulomb-Yang-Mills-Higgs Functional converge to harmonic maps into ${\mathbb S}^2$ extending this data. This asymptotic moreover is subject to concentration conpactness phenomena and the convergence is strong away from a 1 dimensional rectifiable closed concentration set. Then we prove that, having chosen a large enough coupling constant, the limiting minimal energy is converging towards the minimal Brezis-Coron-Lieb relaxed harmonic map energy for this boundary data. In the second part of the paper we examine a different asymptotic regime characterised by overloading monopoles. In this regime we prove that asymptotically, the magnetic field becomes exclusively longitudinal with a $U(1)$ abelian component along the Higgs Field while the Higgs field itself converges to a smooth absolute minimizer of a relaxation of the Faddeev-Skyrme functional of maps from ${\mathbb B}^3$ into ${\mathbb S}^2$. In the third part of the paper we study the behaviour of these configurations when the parameter in front of the Fadeev-Skyrme component respectively goes to zero and $+\infty$. In the first case one recovers the Brezis Coron Lieb relaxed energy at the limit while in the second case the minimal limiting energy is converging towards the minimal Dirichlet energy of maps into ${\mathbb S}^3$ whose projection by the Hopf fibration is equal to the fixed boundary data.

AI.tldr: The paper studies SU(2) Self-Dual Yang-Mills-Higgs in 3D with gauge mass term, analyzing three asymptotic regimes: large mass squeezing monopoles to harmonic maps, overloaded monopoles to Faddeev-Skyrme minimizers, and Faddeev-Skyrme parameter limits recovering various energies.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior of SU(2) Yang-Mills-Higgs systems with gauge mass term, particularly how monopoles behave under different limiting regimes and connect to classical geometric analysis problems like harmonic maps and Faddeev-Skyrme models.
AI.method: Adds gauge mass term (L² norm of connection) to break gauge invariance, forcing Coulomb condition. Studies three asymptotic regimes: 1) large mass squeezing monopoles, 2) overloaded monopoles, 3) Faddeev-Skyrme parameter limits. Uses concentration compactness, harmonic map theory, and variational methods.
AI.result: 1) Large mass: minimizers converge to harmonic maps with concentration on 1D rectifiable set; energy converges to Brezis-Coron-Lieb relaxed harmonic map energy. 2) Overloaded monopoles: magnetic field becomes longitudinal U(1) abelian, Higgs field converges to Faddeev-Skyrme minimizer. 3) Faddeev-Skyrme parameter limits: recovers various energies including Dirichlet energy of maps to S³ via Hopf fibration.
AI.conclusion: The gauge mass term provides a unifying framework connecting Yang-Mills-Higgs systems to classical geometric variational problems. Different asymptotic regimes reveal deep connections between monopole physics, harmonic maps, and topological solitons, with concentration phenomena playing crucial roles in the limiting behavior.

title: C^{1,α} regularity for a class of singular/degenerate fully nonlinear elliptic equations with oblique boundary conditions
summary: In this paper, we establish global $C^{1, α}$ regularity for viscosity solutions to a class of singular and degenerate fully nonlinear elliptic equations subject to oblique boundary conditions. Our work extends the findings in \cite{BKO25} to a broader class of equations, notably encompassing the singular case.

AI.tldr: Extends global C¹,α regularity results for viscosity solutions of singular/degenerate fully nonlinear elliptic equations with oblique boundary conditions, generalizing previous work to include singular cases.
AI.motivation: To generalize existing regularity results (from BKO25) to a broader class of equations, particularly including singular cases that were not covered previously.
AI.method: Establishes global C¹,α regularity for viscosity solutions of singular and degenerate fully nonlinear elliptic equations with oblique boundary conditions, extending prior techniques to handle singular cases.
AI.result: Successfully extends regularity results to a broader class of equations, notably including singular cases that were not addressed in previous work (BKO25).
AI.conclusion: The paper generalizes existing regularity theory to encompass both singular and degenerate fully nonlinear elliptic equations with oblique boundary conditions, filling an important gap in the literature.

title: Sharp upper bounds for the density of relativistic atoms: Noninteracting case
comment: 29 pages. Dedicated to Barry Simon on the occasion of his 80th birthday
summary: We prove an optimal upper bound for the density of electrons of an infinite Bohr atom (no electron-electron interactions) described by the relativistic operators of Chandrasekhar and Dirac. We also consider densities in each angular momentum channel separately.

AI.tldr: Optimal upper bounds for electron density in infinite Bohr atoms using relativistic operators (Chandrasekhar and Dirac), including angular momentum channel analysis.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous mathematical bounds on electron densities in relativistic quantum mechanical systems, specifically for infinite Bohr atoms without electron-electron interactions, using fundamental relativistic operators.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis using relativistic operators (Chandrasekhar and Dirac) to derive optimal upper bounds for electron densities in infinite Bohr atoms, with separate consideration of densities in each angular momentum channel.
AI.result: Proved optimal upper bounds for electron density in infinite Bohr atoms described by relativistic operators, including bounds for densities in individual angular momentum channels.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes fundamental mathematical limits on electron densities in relativistic quantum systems, providing optimal bounds that apply to both Chandrasekhar and Dirac operators, with implications for understanding relativistic atomic structure.

title: Quantum Algorithms for Heterogeneous PDEs: The Neutron Diffusion Eigenvalue Problem
summary: We develop a hybrid classical-quantum algorithm to solve a type of linear reaction-diffusion equation, the neutron diffusion (generalized) k-eigenvalue problem that establishes nuclear criticality. The algorithm handles an equation with piecewise constant coefficients, describing a problem in a heterogeneous medium. We apply uniform finite elements and show that the quantum algorithm provides significant polynomial end-to-end speedup over its classical counterparts. This speedup leverages recent advances in quantum linear systems -- fast inversion and quantum preconditioning -- and uses Hamiltonian simulation as a subroutine. Our results suggest that quantum algorithms may provide speedups for heterogeneous PDEs, though the extent of this advantage over the fastest classical algorithm depends on the effectiveness of other classical approaches such as nonuniform or adaptive meshing for a given problem instance.

AI.tldr: Quantum algorithm for neutron diffusion eigenvalue problems shows polynomial speedup over classical methods using quantum linear systems and Hamiltonian simulation.
AI.motivation: To develop quantum algorithms that can solve linear reaction-diffusion equations (specifically neutron diffusion eigenvalue problems) more efficiently than classical methods, particularly for heterogeneous media problems with piecewise constant coefficients.
AI.method: Hybrid classical-quantum algorithm using uniform finite elements, leveraging quantum linear systems techniques (fast inversion and quantum preconditioning) with Hamiltonian simulation as a subroutine.
AI.result: The quantum algorithm provides significant polynomial end-to-end speedup over classical counterparts for solving the neutron diffusion eigenvalue problem.
AI.conclusion: Quantum algorithms may offer speedups for heterogeneous PDEs, though the advantage depends on effectiveness of classical approaches like nonuniform/adaptive meshing for specific problem instances.

title: Lecture Note for Bounded Controls in Continuous-Time and Control of Several Variables
summary: In this note, we develop the first-order theory of optimal control problems with box constraints on the control. We emphasize the precise modification of Pontryagin's maximum principle when the admissible control set is compact, the projection/clamping formula for scalar quadratic Hamiltonians, the distinction between intrinsic projection inside the optimality system and post hoc truncation of an unconstrained solution, and the corresponding forward-backward sweep implementation. The presentation is pitched at senior PhD students who are already comfortable with variational arguments, adjoint systems, and basic nonlinear analysis. These notes are mainly based on the book ``optimal control applied to biological models'' of Suzanne Lenhart and John T. Workman.

AI.tldr: This paper develops first-order optimal control theory for problems with box constraints on control variables, focusing on modifications to Pontryagin's maximum principle and implementation methods.
AI.motivation: To provide a clear theoretical foundation for optimal control problems with box constraints, addressing the gap between unconstrained theory and practical constrained implementations that are common in applications like biological modeling.
AI.method: Develops modified Pontryagin's maximum principle for compact control sets, analyzes projection/clamping formulas for scalar quadratic Hamiltonians, distinguishes between intrinsic projection within optimality systems versus post hoc truncation, and presents forward-backward sweep implementation methods.
AI.result: Establishes precise theoretical framework for box-constrained optimal control, clarifying the mathematical treatment of control constraints and providing practical implementation guidance for constrained optimization problems.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides essential theoretical tools and implementation strategies for optimal control with box constraints, serving as an educational resource for advanced students working on constrained optimization problems in applied fields like biological modeling.

title: Well-posedness and Hurst parameter estimation for fluid equations driven by fractional transport noise
summary: We study a two-dimensional incompressible vorticity equation on the torus driven by transport-type fractional Brownian noise with Hurst parameter $H \in (1/2,1)$. The model captures persistent, long-range correlated forcing consistent with inertial-range scaling laws and fractional Brownian approximations of turbulent fluctuations. A central ingredient of our approach is a version of the sewing lemma adapted to a class of integrands that includes, but is not limited to, transport-type structures. This result provides a flexible tool for constructing the Young integral and serves as a basis for analysing a wider class of stochastic partial differential equations. Using this approach, we establish existence and uniqueness of solutions via a fixed point argument and investigate statistical properties of the flow. In particular, we study quadratic functionals of the solution and derive an estimator for the Hurst parameter $H$.

AI.tldr: 2D vorticity equation with fractional Brownian transport noise; existence/uniqueness via sewing lemma; Hurst parameter estimation from quadratic functionals.
AI.motivation: Model turbulent fluctuations with long-range correlated forcing using fractional Brownian noise; develop tools for analyzing transport-type SPDEs with rough noise.
AI.method: Adapted sewing lemma for transport-type integrands; Young integral construction; fixed point argument for existence/uniqueness; analysis of quadratic functionals for statistical properties.
AI.result: Established existence and uniqueness of solutions; derived estimator for Hurst parameter H from quadratic functionals of the solution.
AI.conclusion: Developed framework for transport-type SPDEs with fractional Brownian noise; provides tools for statistical analysis including Hurst parameter estimation.
